=== Community Spotlight: Anastasiia Kulakova ===


--- How data for good projects can shape a data scientist ---

By Ioanna Lykiardopoulou

Every month, we spotlight one member for our community . At KickstartAI, we believe that mea

belief: AI agents currently exhibit a kind of design 'collapse' that tends toward bland, homogeneous outputs, requiring human intervention for creativity.
source_quote:
u hadn’t considered. Sometimes the scout gets lost. That’s fine. The point is what you learn for a few cents worth of tokens, not whether you land its exact edits.

To give a concrete example: the physical game ends each round with a short in-character announcement by the host that connects choices to consequences. I wanted a digital moment that felt thematic without turning into a wall of text. I sent a scout to explore end-of-round ideas. It proposed a newspaper front page that highlighted what happened, and it suggested tracking national wealth over rounds with a small chart. The chart was too complex for the target age, but the newspaper idea stuck.


>>> Silent collapse <<<

Andrej Karpathy talks about silent collapse in his conversation with Dwarkesh Patel , which clicked for me. You may be familiar with collapse in GANs . Individual samples look fine, yet together they cover only a narrow slice of what is possible. Karpathy calls this collapse in LLM’s “silent” because it’s not immediately obvious if you look at a single response. But try asking a model for a joke a 100 times, and you get variations of three of the same jokes. This lack of “entropy” limits the creative value that these models offer.

An example of not-so-silent collapse in a GAN model generating faces. The model has failed to capture the true variance of pictures of faces, and so all faces look alike, as you can see below:

In day to day work with coding agents, I feel a version of that in web design. Ask for a new screen and you get the same overused gradients and components. It looks polished in isolation but numbing as a set. This parody at vibe-coded.lol captures this blandness nicely. To my frustration, I encountered this many times while trying to expand the functionality of my frontend screens. Time and again, Codex would “drift” away from the current game design towards slick but cookie-cutter menus, clashing with current game design so far. This means that, for frontend design, I had to really step in and give Codex very small tasks with targeted instructions. Another way to try to fight this collapse was to sometimes switch to Claude Code. While the data these underlying models were trained on overlaps a lot, their tastes are not identical. This would sometimes nudge everything into a more creative direction.


>>> Concluding thoughts <<<

Building this has been humbling. I realized how much creativity went into crafting the original board game, and how even translating it to a browser already forces tough choices. When it comes to experimenting with AI agents, I’ll say it’s definitely been a success. Am I still the one injecting entropy when things start to feel too similar? Yes. But you can definitely use agents to boost creativity: send a cheap agent into the fog, see what it finds, keep only what helps, and do not worry if it gets lost. Finally, if you’re interested in the broader scope of things, Karpathy’s recent conversation with Dwarkesh is a good counterweight to the current agent hype. In fact, if you haven’t seen it yet, why are you reading this slop?

belief: The Netherlands must move from talking about AI to doing AI by focusing less on risks and more on value and concrete applications.
source_quote:
as a physicist, starting his career in strategy consulting, growing into a data specialist, and eventually responsible for the AI organization at Bol. On top of that, he has been on the board of KickstartAI for several years on behalf of Ahold Delhaize.

“Curiosity is my engine,” he says. “I always want to understand how things work: why the sky is blue, why a rainbow curves… those were the questions that kept me busy as a kid. It also explains why I played chess while my friends played football. Chess is actually a perfect metaphor for AI: everyone has the same pieces and the same information, but the difference lies in how you use them. It’s exactly the same with AI.”


>>> From Bol to the National AI Challenge <<<

Webbe spent over ten years at Bol, where he saw how AI can truly transform an organization. Experiments remain useful for exploration, he says, but real impact comes from scaling. “At Bol, dozens of AI applications are now running daily: from delivery forecasts and personal recommendations to logistics optimization and fraud prevention. This transformation has significantly increased operational efficiency and improved the customer experience.”

The experience also sparked a broader idea: if it works there, why not across the whole country? “It’s an ambitious goal,” he acknowledges. “We have leading universities and a rich technological tradition – Wifi, Bluetooth, and Python were all developed or advanced with Dutch contribution – and we have strong companies. Yet globally we perform quite poorly when it comes to adoption. That’s because we focus too much on risks and too little on value. While AI, used wisely, can actually help solve major societal challenges: labor shortages, pressure on healthcare, pressure on cities.”

To illustrate this, he likes to use a homey metaphor: “My daughters, seven and nine, are learning to cut vegetables. First with a protective cover, then with a blunt knife. But at some point they also need to learn to handle a sharp knife. You don’t hide knives because they’re dangerous; you teach children how to use them responsibly. With AI, we must do exactly the same.”


>>> KickstartAI: from talking to doing <<<

That pragmatic perspective is exactly what attracts him to KickstartAI. The foundation, created by KLM, NS, Ahold Delhaize and ING, deliberately focuses on applied AI: projects that don’t live in a lab but in real organizations, with real users. That translation is clearly visible in the projects from recent years. Webbe lists a few, visibly enthusiastic:

“With KLM, for example, we plan more precisely how many meals are needed on board, reducing waste. For NS, we show that abandoned luggage at stations can be detected without tracking individual travelers. At Bol, we predict more accurately how many packages will be processed each day, so delivery vans enter cities less often while half-empty. And at ING, we’re building a multilingual chatbot for financial health that helps people with financial questions in clear, understandable language.”

And all of this with only twenty employees. Webbe smiles. “People often underestimate

belief: AI initiatives should not be treated as standalone strategies; they must be anchored in concrete frictions and organisational goals.
source_quote:
ment. The project, winner of the National AI Challenge 2025, has internally been nicknamed Al Capone.

The reference is telling: the gangster was not convicted of murder, but of tax evasion.

“You see that principle in environmental cases as well”, Niknam explains. “The most serious violation is not always the easiest to prove. Sometimes it’s more effective to revoke a permit or shut down a company than to wait for a drawn-out criminal trial.”

The AI tool only supports the initial analysis phase. An intelligence officer enters context, a report, an anomaly, a suspicion. The system searches accessible legislation, case law and similar cases, but it does not issue judgments.

“You want to see more quickly where the legal opening might be. After that, it remains human work.”

Niknam stresses that he is not responsible for the overall AI policy of the police. “I’m speaking here in a personal capacity.” Yet the questions he encounters, about responsibility, boundaries and legitimacy, are broader ones. Within the police, AI is not introduced lightly, he says.

“We operate in a constitutional state. That requires explainability, proportionality and human judgment.”

It also means that not everything that is technically possible is automatically desirable.

“The police are vigilant and serve the values of the democratic rule of law. That is our mission, and it must always come first.”


--- The broader lesson ---

This is also the lesson he wants to share with other organizations. “Never let AI become a strategy in itself. It should always be a means.”

In his own work, the starting point was very concrete: the slow start of complex environmental investigations. Not reforming the entire police organization, but addressing one clear bottleneck.

According to him, that’s where things often go wrong. Organizations start with the technology instead of the friction.

“The danger is that new technology ends up reinforcing the very problem you’re trying to solve”, he says.

He also sees how many organizations are still structured linearly, according to the model of the industrial revolution: step one, then step two, then step three. That worked in factories, but in knowledge work it leads to waiting times, handovers and layers of control.

“You end up with gatekeepers everywhere. Another check, another form. And the system gradually clogs up.”

The temptation is to replace those gatekeepers with AI. “But that’s like buying a better mop while the tap is still running.”


--- Building blocks instead of chains ---

According to Niknam, the real intervention lies in the design of the process itself. Why must work constantly be handed over? Why not organize it more asynchronously? He advocates working with modular building blocks, like a house where you can renovate one room without demolishing the entire building.

In such a system, AI is a tool, not a load-bearing wall. “AI must be replaceable. Switchable off when the context changes. If you can’t disconnect it without everything collapsing, you have a problem.

Because if the stru

--- AI lessons from the field ---


=== Why focus and technology go hand in hand ===


>>> ING's COO Risk Leon Dusée on AI in risk management, why pilots often stall, and how a promising AI solution such as a financial health assistant could help people sooner <<<

By Rutger Huizenga

AI is now on almost every agenda. Not as a gadget, but as a strategic question: where do you apply it, how do you scale it, and how do you prevent experiments from getting stuck in pilot phase? In the interview series "AI Lessons from the Field," we speak with leaders who have to make those choices in practice. Today: Leon Dusée, Global Head Chief Operating Officer Risk at ING. “AI can add value in thousands of places, but if you start everywhere, you often end up nowhere.”

When speaking with Leon Dusée about the future of banking, it quickly becomes clear that he is not only focused on the latest technological gadgets. Although, as COO Risk at ING, he is responsible for the security and stability of a global digital infrastructure, his focus lies on a surprisingly human aspect: making deliberate choices.

“AI can add value in thousands of places,” he says matter-of-factly. “But that is exactly the danger. If you start everywhere, you often end up nowhere.”

It is a lesson he learned in practice at the bank, where he started eight years ago as a management trainee and rose to global leadership of the operations connected to risk management. Technological choices become reality faster when the entire organization has the right focus.

“Everyone sees opportunities in AI, and those opportunities are real. But in a large organization, the temptation is strong to experiment in many places at once. Before long, you get a proliferation of pilots, proofs of concept, and isolated ideas that never truly land in the core process. The art is to say: this is where we will invest, and this is where we will not. That sounds simple, but it is not. Because it also means saying no to good ideas, to initiatives that may seem smarter or more efficient in themselves.”


--- Critical thinking ---

In the operations domain of Risk, for which he is responsible, Dusée clearly sees the difference AI can make. Much of the work involves assessing huge volumes of informa

belief: Starting AI initiatives from the technology rather than a clear problem is a common and counterproductive trap.

Post 7: We are proud to share that our CEO, Erick Webbe, will be taking part in a roundtable discussion at the Dutch Parliament (Tweede Kamer der Staten-Generaal) on 26 March about optimizing AI opportunities for the Netherlands.

At KickstartAI, our position is clear: The Netherlands 🇳🇱  has many prerequisites in place – talent, infrastructure, and a strong business climate – but we are losing momentum to low AI implementation capacity and structures that weren't built for the speed of this technology. As a result, we're not realizing the full potential of AI and its benefits for society.

Erick will be advocating for a national shift from talk to action. To do that, we need:
✅ Space for execution and a mandate for practical experimentation. 
✅ Moving past existing structures to prioritize performance and scale. 
✅ Bridging the impact gap by tackling today’s most complex challenges

part in a roundtable discussion at the Dutch Parliament (Tweede Kamer der Staten-Generaal) on 26 March about optimizing AI opportunities for the Netherlands.

At KickstartAI, our position is clear: The Netherlands 🇳🇱  has many prerequisites in place – talent, infrastructure, and a strong business climate – but we are losing momentum to low AI implementation capacity and structures that weren't built for the speed of this technology. As a result, we're not realizing the full potential of AI and its benefits for society.

Erick will be advocating for a national shift from talk to action. To do that, we need:

Post 3: On Friday, we wrapped up our last workshop of the year with a hands-on session focused on humanitarian impact 🌎 

We teamed up with 510 - The Netherlands Red Cross' data & digital team to tackle a critical challenge: the lack of reliable data on water access and waste management in areas with limited monitoring.

Participants explored how openly available satellite imagery could help detect:
🔹 Water points such as wells, tanks, and reservoirs
🔹 Indicators of pollution or poor waste management

💡 The outcome?
Rather than building full models, the goal was to assess feasibility. Mixed teams brainstormed patterns visible from space and identified the tools needed to turn these insights into support for humanitarian and environmental action.

A big thank you to The Hague Humanity Hub for hosting us!

Post 7: On Friday we got together for our last KickstartAI meetup of the year, an evening focused on the intersection of AI and space. ☄️ 

A big thank you to everyone who joined, to The Hague Tech for hosting us, and to our guest speakers for sharing their expertise on this fascinating topic: 

🔸 Michael Janßen (Radboud University & Event Horizon Telescope)
shared his work on a neural network trained on millions of simulated datasets – showing how it overcomes observational challenges to extract complex scientific parameters (such as spin and magnetic fields) from the Event Horizon Telescope's black hole data.

 explained how multi-agent swarms, like drones and rovers, use edge intelligence to navigate and make decisions in challenging environments. He highlighted Bayesian methods, sensor fusion, and recent developments in multi-agent space systems, including TU Delft’s Lunar Zebro hexapod.

Post 9: 🎧 In our latest podcast, Evertjan Peer talks with Marieke Meeske (researcher at the Zero Hunger Lab at Tilburg University) about how NLP is being used in food security analysis and policymaking.

One finding from her research is hard to ignore:
🔍 Across 60+ NLP use cases in food security, only 12% ever reach deployment.
The other 88% remain in the research phase – indicating a clear implementation gap.

Marieke explains what’s behind this gap and why collaborations between academia and societal partners are essential if we want these methods to have impact.

Post 10: We’re excited to share that Erick Webbe will be joining KickstartAI as our new CEO starting January 1 🚀 

Erick is a familiar and trusted force within KickstartAI. As a long-time board member representing Ahold Delhaize, he has actively supported our mission to accelerate the adoption of AI in the Netherlands. He brings a deep understanding of how large organizations work, how AI moves from idea to implementation, and what it takes to connect different stakeholders around one shared goal.

In his new role as CEO, Erick will focus on growing KickstartAI’s impact: helping more organisations tackle concrete AI challenges, sharing what works openly with the community, and strengthening the Dutch AI ecosystem. 🇳🇱 

As Erick puts it, “the Netherlands must move from talking about AI to doing AI.”
“We want to show that progress doesn’t start with big words, but with tangible actions. And that a small country, if it wants to, can set a big example. I’m incredibly excited to turn our mission into reality.”

🌟 We’re looking forward to this next chapter with Erick guiding the way!

Post 16: Yesterday, ING and KickstartAI - together with our partners Ahold Delhaize, KLM Royal Dutch Airlines and Nederlandse Spoorwegen - hosted the AI team of the Ministry of Economic Affairs for a working visit focused on practical AI application and adoption.

🇳🇱 KickstartAI is built on the belief that accelerating the Dutch economy through AI requires shared learning and action. During the session, our founding partners shared practical insights from the AI projects we’re carrying out together: 
🔸 What it really takes to move from idea to implementation and adoption
🔸  How to set clear goals, and how to embed AI into the core of their organisations - not as side projects, but as drivers of impact.

We discussed how organisations can strengthen their AI adoption by learning through doing, by looking at AI not only as a technology but also in terms of organisational change and broader impact, and by integrating it deeply into customer-facing and operational processes. 

We also explored how the government can help lower barriers and accelerate this transition.

⚡ Our priority at KickstartAI is clear: the urgency of AI adoption is real, and action is needed now for the Netherlands to stay competitive, economically resilient, and technologically independent.

Thanks to the Ministry for the open and constructive exchange, and to our partners for sharing their experiences so openly. The work continues!

#AI #AINetherlands #AIAdoption

Post 17: 🇳🇱 Today, a broad coalition of AI experts presents the National AI Delta Plan – a concrete roadmap to make the Netherlands a European leader in AI through targeted investments in knowledge, talent, and infrastructure.

At KickstartAI, we strongly support this initiative and actively contribute to its vision. For years, we’ve been working hands-on with companies across sectors to move AI from experimentation to scalable impact, while cultivating one of the Netherlands’ biggest communities of AI professionals. 

📉 The Netherlands currently ranks 23rd worldwide in AI application. Without immediate and ambitious action, we risk falling further behind, with major consequences for our competitiveness, economic security, and technological sovereignty.

We believe the AI Delta Plan is essential as it calls for: 
 ✅ Accelerating AI adoption in businesses and government
 ✅ Investing in compute infrastructure and AI labs
 ✅ Attracting and training AI talent
 ✅ Supporting AI startups and scaleups
 ✅ Establishing an independent National AI Institute
 ✅ Addressing regulatory friction that limits AI adoption and startup growth 

We are committed to contributing to this mission by accelerating responsible AI adoption, fostering talent, and strengthening collaboration across the ecosystem. The time to act is

Post 1: Afgelopen dinsdag organiseerden NLTech, KickstartAI en Techleap hét Nederlandse Tech verkiezingsdebat, waar kandidaat-Kamerleden en ondernemers in gesprek gingen over een toekomstbestendige digitale economie en sterk tech-ecosysteem.
 
Kandidaat-Kamerleden Wimar Bolhuis (GroenLinks-PvdA), Maes van Lanschot (CDA), Claire Martens - America (VVD), Joost Sneller (D66), Marieke Koekkoek (Volt Nederland) en Christian van der Krift, PhD (ChristenUnie) gingen het debat aan over hoe Nederland een toekomstbestendig tech ecosysteem —  van startups tot digitale kampioenen — kan bouwen en hoe we als Nederland AI-koploper kunnen worden.
 
Wat opviel tijdens de discussie over #kapitaal: vrijwel alle partijen pleitten voor meer langetermijndenken en investeringszekerheid. Niemand twijfelde aan de noodzaak om innovatie te stimuleren, maar de vraag bleef 𝘩𝘰𝘦. Er werd gepleit voor het versterken van de Europese #kapitaalunie, maar ook voor een nationale #investeringsinstelling en meer publiek-private samenwerking.
 
Onderwijs en kennisontwikkeling vormen de basis van onze toekomstige economie. Tegelijkertijd benadrukte de partijen dat fiscale regelingen als de expat-regeling cruciaal zijn voor het aantrekken van internationaal #talent, en dat aanpassing hiervan onverstandig is voor de Nederlandse concurrentiepositie binnen de EU.
 
#AI werd gezien als essentieel. VVD, GLPvdA, Volt, D66 en CU waren het eens: voor Nederland is het nu tijd voor actie om concurrerend te blijven en kansen te grijpen door snelle (en verantwoorde) AI-adoptie.

👉 Belangrijk is nu dat we deze mooie plannen en ambities terugzien in het regeerakkoord. 

#AI #AINederland

Post 12: A massive thank you to everyone who came out to our AI in Finance meetup last night! It was fantastic to talk tech, share a cocktail, and catch up. We're also very grateful to Eraneos Netherlands for partnering with us to make the event happen.

For those who couldn't make it, or for anyone who wants a recap, here are three key takeaways from the evening:

1️⃣ Infrastructure is the foundation: Federico Calore (ING) shared the blueprint for ING's next-gen data platform, VISTA, proving that scaling advanced AI and GenAI requires a fundamental, cloud-first architectural shift built on principles of governance and security.
2️⃣ AI for compliance is here: Gergely Bognár (Eraneos) demonstrated a GenAI framework that automates legal compliance checks for the Dutch pension market, ensuring both accuracy and completeness against legislation.
3️⃣ Transparency is non-negotiable: Tim Kleinloog (Deeploy) highlighted that in the fight against financial crime, AI models must be transparent, fair, and explainable to be truly effective and trustworthy in a regulated world.

💡 The big theme of the night was clear: we're moving from AI hype to practical, responsible implementation. 

It was a great way to close out the summer season, and now we're all fired up and ready to roll up our sleeves for the busy months ahead. See you all at the next one! 

Next up: 
AI x Education: Rewiring learning, Thursday, September 18 in Amsterdam

Post 13: 💡 To unlock AI’s potential, we have to stop treating it like another IT upgrade. We have to rethink how we work and operate.

As Sophia Claire Zitman pointed out at TNW Conference, AI is too big to comprehend and too different in nature from what we’re used to. And that is why so many organizations struggle to turn potential into impact.

They’re blocked by misaligned incentives. They keep approaching AI as a technology rollout — when in reality, it’s a shift in mindset. It’s not about hiring more developers or running more pilots. Nor about using traditional metrics to measure success.

👉 It’s about the people who are ready to reimagine what’s possible — and act on it. That’s exactly what we do at KickstartAI.

#AI #AIadoptionNetherlands #KickstartAI

Post 14: 🧠 The smartest thing humans do? Ignore most of the data.

In our latest podcast, Evertjan Peer, Cascha van Wanrooij, and Paul Kuyer explore what sets human intelligence apart from AI — and why that difference matters in practice.

AI learns by consuming massive amounts of data. But humans evolved to ignore most information, and act on the small fraction that matters the most.

The contrast goes beyond how we process information. It also shapes what we prioritize, remember, and understand, which influences how we behave. The AI systems we’re currently using have different foundations:
🔎  Current AI architectures don't have an integrated, persistent (and autobiographical) long-term memory
⚙️ Current models operate without intent or self-awareness
📉 But they’re still being used to generate decisions and influence at scale

So what kind of guardrails do we need when deploying systems that lack the constraints and instincts that shaped human cognition?

#TheKickstartAIPodcast #AI #ArtificialIntelligence

Post 4: Last Thursday, our CEO Erick Webbe joined the roundtable discussion in the Dutch Parliament (Tweede Kamer der Staten-Generaal) on how the Netherlands can make better use of the opportunities AI offers. 🇳🇱 

Our view is clear: the urgency is there – now execution needs to follow.
As Erick put it: "We need to take AI out of the ivory tower and into practice.”

That broader message was reflected in several parts of the discussion. As Maarten de Rijke also noted, the Netherlands has spent a long time thinking through the rules around AI; now it is time to start playing.

The Netherlands already has a strong foundation – from long-term research through ELLIS - European Laboratory for Learning and Intelligent Systems, to mid-term collaboration through ICAI - Innovation Center for Artificial Intelligence labs, to short-cycle implementation through initiatives like KickstartAI. What is still missing is not ideas, talent, or infrastructure, but turning that capacity into actual use.

The next step is to build on what already works. As @Petra Piersma noted, that means focusing on scaling proven solutions. 📊 

That’s the part we focus on at KickstartAI: working with organisations to apply AI in practice and sharing the learnings so they can be replicated across the Dutch ecosystem. 

We’d be happy to welcome interested MPs Claire Martens - America, Henk Vermeer, Ouafa Oualhadj, Jantine Zwinkels, Sarah El Boujdaini, Barbara Kathmann to one of our upcoming project launches and show what that real life AI solutions look like in action. 🚀  

#VVD #CDA #BBB #D66 #PRO #KickstartAI #AI

Post 7: Great news! 🚀 We collaborated with DenkWerk on their new report examining how the Netherlands can better prepare for an increasingly interconnected threat landscape.

🔎 The challenge: 
Traditional threat analysis treats risks in isolation. But today, threats are deeply intertwined. A cyberattack can disrupt supply chains. Climate events can ripple into economic shocks. As COVID showed, health crises can spark nationwide consequences.

💡 Our contribution: 
Thanks to the work of our ML Engineer Cascha van Wanrooij, we developed a node analysis tool that maps how threats connect and amplify each other.

We used LLMs to extract causal relationships from thousands of government and research documents — creating a semantic network of interlinked national threats. The result: an interactive graph that helps policymakers explore these complex threat relationships visually, moving beyond traditional "probability × impact" models to include interconnectedness as a crucial third dimension. (Link to our tool: https://lnkd.in/dqej7mDQ)

👉 Read the full report here: https://lnkd.in/d7ABqq7f

#ResilienceByDesign #AIforGood #KickstartAI

Post 8: Yesterday at TNW Conference, our very own Sophia Claire Zitman, addressed why AI adoption is lagging despite its €85bn potential for the Dutch economy. The problem isn't the tech or talent; it's that AI is too big to fully grasp.

As Sophia said: “AI isn’t digital transformation. It's not about automating things. It is about rewriting, reshaping, and rethinking the way you work.” 

To realize its potential, we must go beyond traditional metrics like OKRs and KPIs. They simply don’t work. The key is finding the entrepreneurial spirits and change-makers within your organizations — and enabling them to break the mould.  

⚙️ In our projects, this approach has led to real results:
🥗 KLM Royal Dutch Airlines cut food waste by 65%
📦 bol improved parcel delivery efficiency by 6%
🚉 Nederlandse Spoorwegen train stations became safer

Let's stop forcing AI into old frameworks — and embrace the change it brings. 

#KickstartAI #TNWConference2025 #AIadoptionNetherlands

Post 18: ⚠️ What are we going to do to start AI adoption tomorrow?
That’s the key question our CEO Nils Beers posed during his speech at the Netherlands’ Tech Election Debate we co-organized last week.

At KickstartAI, we believe AI isn’t a blocker — it’s a force for good. It can safeguard our welfare state, boost our economy, and strengthen our society. The urgency is clear: if we hesitate, the Netherlands risks falling behind in competitiveness and sovereignty.

That’s why there’s no more time for endless roundtable talks, boardroom discussions, and debates in The Hague. 

It’s time to:
🔸Boost public investment by supporting frontrunners from business and from the tech ecosystem
🔸Adopt rules that support entrepreneurs instead of overburdening them with compliance and uncertainty
🔸Focus on measurable impact instead of AI pilots
🔸Create space for the doers and enable them to share their learnings

🚀 We need to act now. 
🤝 Let’s build a coalition of doing, together.

#AI #AIadoption #Coalitionofthedoing #AIadoptieNederland

Post 12: A massive thank you to everyone who came out to our AI in Finance meetup last night! It was fantastic to talk tech, share a cocktail, and catch up. We're also very grateful to Eraneos Netherlands for partnering with us to make the event happen.

For those who couldn't make it, or for anyone who wants a recap, here are three key takeaways from the evening:

1️⃣ Infrastructure is the foundation: Federico Calore (ING) shared the blueprint for ING's next-gen data platform, VISTA, proving that scaling advanced AI and GenAI requires a fundamental, cloud-first architectural shift built on principles of governance and security.
2️⃣ AI for compliance is here: Gergely Bognár (Eraneos) demonstrated a GenAI framework that automates legal compliance checks for the Dutch pension market, ensuring both accuracy and completeness against legislation.
3️⃣ Transparency is non-negotiable: Tim Kleinloog (Deeploy) highlighted that in the fight against financial crime, AI models must be transparent, fair, and explainable to be truly effective and trustworthy in a regulated world.

💡 The big theme of the night was clear: we're moving from AI hype to practical, responsible implementation. 

It was a great way to close out the summer season, and now we're all fired up and ready to roll up our sleeves for the busy months ahead. See you all at the next one! 

Next up: 
AI x Education: Rewiring learning, Thursday, September 18 in Amsterdam

belief: KickstartAI sees knowledge sharing and storytelling as essential infrastructure for the Dutch AI ecosystem’s growth.
source_quote:
What’s your background, and how did you enter the AI industry? <<<

I fell in love with languages and the ancient world at a very young age, so I decided to study Classics (Ancient Greek and Latin language and literature). After my studies, I worked as a teacher for about six years, before debating whether I’d move on to a career in academia or pursue a career in writing and journalism.

I opted for the second option, and in 2021, I joined TNW as a tech journalist — that was my first contact with the technology world. At first, I wasn’t convinced this world would be the right fit for me. But it didn’t take too long for my skepticism to turn into fascination, especially for science-based technologies or technologies that support social good.

I covered the European tech and startup scene for four years, with a focus on AI, deep tech, healthtech, and policy. So when KickstartAI came across my path, it felt like a natural step.


>>> What do you enjoy about working here? <<<

The team! It’s great to be surrounded by people who aren’t just intelligent, but also very kind and fun to be around. I’m learning a lot from them.


>>> What drives you in your work? <<<

Telling stories that matter and translating complex topics is definitely one part. Researching and learning is another. I’m also very mission-driven, so working for an organization that’s using technology for good is a big motivating factor.


>>> How does your work impact the broader AI industry in the Netherlands? <<<

I believe sharing knowledge, insights, and important lessons is essential for the AI ecosystem. If we want to grow, we need to learn from each other and disseminate information that everyone can benefit from.


>>> What’s an AI field that excites you? <<<

I love the intersection of language and AI, be it translation technology or multilingual LLMs and the intricacies of language diversity. Lately, I’ve been reading a lot about AI’s use in text restoration . I can’t wait for the time when models will be able to decode undeciphered ancient scripts, such as Linear A!


>>> What’s your advice to people considering a career in AI? <<<

If the field interests you, there’s a role for you in it. The AI industry needs people from different backgrounds to help shape how we develop and use this technology. The more voices contribute, the more we can move towards human-centric AI. My advice is: understand your skills and how you can apply them in this space.


>>> Fun fact about you? <<<

I’ve read the Harry Potter books so many times that I remember entire passages by heart! And yes, as a kid, I was very disappointed I never got a letter from Hogwarts.

💡 Want to learn more about KickstartAI and our mission to accelerate AI adoption in the Netherlands? Check out our website , explore our open positions , or connect with Ioanna on LinkedIn !

Thank you for subscribing to our newsletter!

====================================================================================================
URL: https://kickstart.ai/news/drawbacks-benchmark-driven-ai-research
========================

Post 6: Last Friday, the KickstartAI community gathered at the Hortus Botanicus Amsterdam to start 2026 by (re)connecting with each other and discussing our work in AI 🤝 

Through facilitated networking rounds, people quickly got specific about their roles, current challenges, and what kind of collaboration would actually be useful. 

From there, Roel Willems (Ahold Delhaize) offered an update on the state of AI adoption in the Netherlands – a timely reminder that progress isn’t just about technical capability, but about making deliberate choices along the way.

We ended with a guided walk through the gardens, which turned out to be a fitting metaphor: healthy ecosystems depend on diversity, cross-pollination, and long-term thinking – much like the AI ecosystem we’re building. 

🌟 A big thank you to everyone who joined! 

#KickstartAICommunity #AINetherlands #AI

Post 7: 🌍 How are AI and Earth observation changing decision-making on the ground?

Public space data, commercial satellite imagery, and recent advances in AI are expanding what’s possible with Earth observation – both in how data is accessed and how it’s analysed.

In our latest article, we look at current and evolving AI uses in EO and examine different EO markets and applications, from climate monitoring and biodiversity assessment to regional policy-making and emerging commercial use cases. 

We spoke with Parya Pasha Zadeh (Program Manager at NL Space Campus) and Thijs Oosterhuis (Solutions Architect for Geo and Remote Sensing at @ Provincie Zuid-Holland) about how these developments are unfolding in practice, drawing on perspectives from applied EO work in the Netherlands.

👇 Read the full article below:
https://lnkd.in/esPQitmu

#AI #EarthObservation #GIS

Post 8: 💡 How can AI help detect ink on 2,000-year-old carbonized texts?

This question is at the core of the Vesuvius Challenge — a global competition to virtually unroll and read the Herculaneum scrolls buried by Mt. Vesuvius in 79 AD.

🎧 In our latest podcast, Evertjan Peer, Cascha van Wanrooij, and Ioanna Lykiardopoulou explain how a team of university students trained a model to detect ink by producing a segmentation map from the CT scans of the burned papyri.

But how did they make sure that the model wasn’t hallucinating? And why did they choose TimeSformer for training?

👉 Listen to the full discussion here: https://lnkd.in/eWcaJkJx

#TheKickstartAIpodcast #AI #VesuviusChallenge

Post 3: At the Dutch AI Congress this week, we had the opportunity to share key insights on how to develop and implement AI solutions in healthcare organizations.

For the past seven months, we’ve been working closely with Erasmus MC to predict patient inflow at the emergency department — a step toward improving efficiency, capacity management, and staffing.

Our Tech Lead, Evertjan P., took the stage to talk about what it really takes to apply AI in hospital operational processes. Here are three of the most important lessons we've learned so far:

💡 Thoroughly understand and define the problem you’re aiming to solve:
This is key to creating a specific goal and a concrete plan.

💡 Assess feasibility: 
What data sources are available, and what level of performance can an AI model achieve based on these? How can we use these AI-driven insights in day-to-day practice?

💡 Determine the processes for tech integration and user adoption:
Ask: What’s the IT infrastructure like? How will the AI tool be presented to end-users? How should it be used in practice?

We’re proud to be part of the AI movement in the Netherlands — and excited for what’s next. 🧡 

A big shoutout to the AI Coalitie 4 Nederland | AIC4NL for organizing this inspiring congress!

#AI #HealthcareAI #HealthcareInnovation

Post 4: Very proud to announce that we once again joined forces with AI Coalitie 4 Nederland | AIC4NL to accelerate AI adoption in the Netherlands. Frans Muller, CEO of Ahold Delhaize, one of the founding partner companies of KickstartAI, today unveiled the second edition of the National AI Challenge, the flagship initiative of our renewed collaboration. 

The National AI Challenge was first introduced by KickstartAI and AI Coalition 4 NL in 2024, aiming to solve  concrete challenges in businesses and social organizations with artificial intelligence. The winner of the first edition, Erasmus MC, has worked with KickstartAI over the past year to improve efficiency and quality of care in the Emergency Department using AI technology.

This year’s National AI Challenge will start at the end of April and will select not one, but two use cases.

Read more about our collaboration in our press release https://lnkd.in/d-MnC-Vc  and stay tuned for more details about how to participate in the Challenge!

Pieter Jeekel Nils Beers Tjarda Voorneman Frans Muller Keely Richardson Klaas Wagenaar

Post 15: What did the great minds in AI say about 2025’s progress – and what do they predict for the future?

🎙️ In our latest podcast, Cascha van Wanrooij, Evertjan Peer, and Nic van Vliet reflect on why leading AI thinkers interpret last year's developments so differently: from post-scaling researchers like Ilya Sutskever calling for new ideas, to fundamental sceptics such as Richard Sutton questioning today's dominant approaches, and to lab leaders like Demis Hassabis continuing to see scaling as the main driver of progress. 

How do these differences carry over to views on AGI?

🎧 Listen to the full conversation here: https://lnkd.in/e9TTVz9G

#AI #AGI #AIResearch #TheKickstartAIPodcast

Post 16: In an interconnected economy, shocks rarely occur in isolation and can reinforce each other, escalating into broader crises.

In this ESB article below, our ML engineer Cascha van Wanrooij, together with Barbara Baarsma and Ruth Smalbraak, analyze what this means for economic risks and resilience in the Netherlands. The article draws on a meta-analysis of more than 5,000 policy documents, using AI to identify and map interdependencies, as part of DenkWerk’s 2025 report: “Resilience by design – the five principles for a resilient Netherlands.”

👉 Link to the full analysis: https://lnkd.in/ejk3h7F7

Post 3: On Friday, we wrapped up our last workshop of the year with a hands-on session focused on humanitarian impact 🌎 

We teamed up with 510 - The Netherlands Red Cross' data & digital team to tackle a critical challenge: the lack of reliable data on water access and waste management in areas with limited monitoring.

Participants explored how openly available satellite imagery could help detect:
🔹 Water points such as wells, tanks, and reservoirs
🔹 Indicators of pollution or poor waste management

💡 The outcome?
Rather than building full models, the goal was to assess feasibility. Mixed teams brainstormed patterns visible from space and identified the tools needed to turn these insights into support for humanitarian and environmental action.

A big thank you to The Hague Humanity Hub for hosting us!

We're kicking off the new year with a Community Meet & Greet brunch, you can RSVP here: https://lnkd.in/e-GEdG_J

Post 4: 🎧 Our new podcast episode is out, and it’s our 2025 AI roundup! 

Evertjan Peer, Cascha van Wanrooij, and Nic van Vliet look back at the models and moments that shaped this year, from the releases of DeepSeek R1, o3, Gemini 3, and Claude 4 to the rise of coding agents, and the push towards multimodality. 

They discuss why 2025 became the year of gradual progress rather then the year of general agents, and analyze the different views of today’s leading AI thinkers – from Ilya Sutskever’s push for a new age of research to Dario Amodei’s scaling maximalism. They wrap up with market predictions for 2026.

👇 Listen to the full conversation below, and share your thoughts in the comments! 

#TheKickstartAIPodcast #AI2025Roundup #AI

belief: Startup founders should neither romanticise entrepreneurship nor remain indecisive; they must consciously commit and then act.
source_quote:
“When transitioning to a sales-led enterprise play, for example, you need to think of a different organizational structure, with a focus on customer success, solutions engineers, and dedicated account executives,” he says.

He compares this to a product-led growth strategy, where the focus shifts towards growth marketing and community management.

Lleverage itself is now in-between the sales-led and the product-led growth motion.

“We’re consciously not making a decision right now,” Lennard explains. “But we’re going to make one in the following months. We can’t maintain this position for a long time, because it defocuses a small company like us.”

Besides market fit, Lennard mentions another challenge for early-stage companies: funding. The startup raised €3mn earlier this month, bringing its total investment to €5mn in less than a year. It has managed to avoid the pitfalls of the funding rollercoaster thanks to its seasoned founders who have already built and scaled successful companies.

But for first-time founders, attracting capital — especially in the AI space — is no easy task, Lennard says. “You have to convince VCs about the defensibility of your idea and that you can actually outgrow your competition.”


=== Building a startup is no bed of roses ===

When asked about the biggest lesson he’s learned on his journey with Lleverage, Lennard doesn’t hesitate: “I had forgotten how painful it is to build a startup.”

He cautions prospective founders against romanticizing the concept of a startup. “Make sure you actually want it and talk to people who have done it before or are doing it now.”

At the same time, he advises against inaction. “If you really want it, just go for it, because sitting on the fence for eternity is also something you shouldn’t do.”

Another important lesson for Lennard is figuring out how to handle competition — especially in a space as crowded and fast-moving as AI.

“I don’t open my LinkedIn every day anymore, because every time I do, I see another competitor,” he jokes. He explains that being confronted with competition every day is not only draining, but also distracting.

“You have to find the right balance between listening to those noises and just believe in what you’re building. There’s a lot of value in perseverance.”


=== Growing the Dutch AI ecosystem needs more work ===

Lennard contrasts the Netherlands with France, where active government support, investor focus, and ambitious infrastructure — like the soon-to-launch world’s largest startup campus — are transforming the country into Europe’s AI powerhouse.

“I feel in the last six months we’re making steps, but before that, there wasn’t a lot of noise around the ecosystem,” he says. “I think we can still do more.”

Lennard says that initiatives like KickstartAI have a role to play. He joined the community when he attended one of our events earlier this year. “I think that communities can be a big boost to the ecosystem and they’re a great way for people to feel that they are part of something.”

💡 Want to learn more about the KickstartAI community and our mission to accelerate AI adoption in the Nether

belief: Perseverance and selective attention to competition are essential to sustaining focus in crowded, fast‑moving AI markets.

Lennard says that initiatives like KickstartAI have a role to play. He joined the community when he attended one of our events earlier this year. “I think that communities can be a big boost to the ecosystem and they’re a great way for people to feel that they are part of something.”

The Netherlands has made visible progress in embedding ethical governance into AI development, especially in the banking sector, says Govender-Ropert, speaking from her personal experience in ING and Rabobank.

But challenges remain. She points to a lack of public trust and the need for AI education.

Trust isn’t the only challenge. Captain van den Broek sees ambition in the defense sector — with rising investment, collaborations, and experimentation — but acknowledges that the Netherlands is still in a catch-up phase. “Swarming autonomous tanks don’t exist yet,” he notes. “We know we need to make major steps in this field.”

Iskander Hannivoort is more blunt: “There’s a huge backlog.” He says Dutch organizations still underestimate security — not just in AI, but across the board. “The Netherlands is a safe country, and people aren’t used to violence or danger.”

Cybersecurity researcher Stjepan Picek adds that while the Netherlands is not lagging behind its neighboring countries, it isn’t leading either. “We’re in the middle. There’s good research on AI for security, but much less on the security of AI itself,” he says. Bridging the gap between academic research and real-world application is still an ongoing struggle.


--- Advice for AI professionals: ---

Stay educated: AI and cybersecurity evolve rapidly. Professionals must dedicate ongoing effort to stay informed about emerging threats, tools, and best practices.

belief: Executives and leaders must personally experiment with AI tools to make informed strategic and investment decisions.
source_quote:
amount of food waste, because far less has to be thrown away. So it is a double win.”


--- Trust and explainability ---

Another example Slagt is proud of concerns a joint project on contingency fuel : the additional fuel an aircraft carries for emergencies and unexpected circumstances. It naturally requires a delicate balance. Safety always comes first, but carrying too much fuel is also unsustainable and costly. “If you need it, it has to be on board. But you also do not want to take too much, because it adds unnecessary weight.”

Together with KickstartAI, KLM built a model that takes a higher number of variables into account than the traditional approach. Think of load, passenger numbers, wind, weather systems, and conditions at the destination. It is a unique model, but “ultimately, the captain is responsible and decides how much fuel is taken on board. So it is important that you can explain how such a model works, which variables go into it, and why the advice comes out the way it does.”


--- Knowledge ---

And that, according to Slagt, is exactly where things often go wrong. “A model may look excellent on paper, but if people do not understand it, nothing changes on the work floor.” That is precisely why it is important for end users (in this case pilots as well as the pilots’ union,) to be involved early in the development of such an application. And it is equally important for executives to understand both the capabilities and the limitations of AI. Slagt says: “If you have to make decisions about investments, risks, and applications, you cannot follow this from a distance. You really have to immerse yourself in what AI can and cannot do.”

At KLM, that meant the leadership team did not get yet another presentation, but instead had to sit down behind a laptop themselves, in this case at Google’s office. Slagt: “The assignment was simple: build your own AI assistant for an advertising campaign. Or create an agent that scans aviation news every week and puts it in your inbox. Only when you do it yourself do you see what AI can  deliver.”

KLM noticed the same thing when generative AI started advancing rapidly and employees wanted to start working with it. That is why KLM built Talia: a secure environment in which employees can safely work with models such as Gemini, Mistral, and ChatGPT. “It is important to keep building the bridge between the theory behind AI and its practical application. The colleagues that will work with it need to understand it, embrace it, and gain trust in it.”


--- The Dutch debate ---

But according to Slagt, the learning doesn't stop there. Anyone who really wants to make serious progress with AI also has to look outside their company.For that reason, KLM is active in KickstartAI, an initiative it co-founded with NS, Ahold, and ING. For Slagt, the value lies not only in the technology, but also in the way of collaborating: companies, sectors, and specialists coming together to solve real problems and put AI on the map in the Netherlands.

He calls KickstartAI, not without reason, “the coalition of the doing.” It is not a network of endless meetings and discussion groups, but a founda

Kandidaat-Kamerleden Wimar Bolhuis (GroenLinks-PvdA), Maes van Lanschot (CDA), Claire Martens - America (VVD), Joost Sneller (D66), Marieke Koekkoek (Volt Nederland) en Christian van der Krift, PhD (ChristenUnie) gingen het debat aan over hoe Nederland een toekomstbestendig tech ecosysteem —  van startups tot digitale kampioenen — kan bouwen en hoe we als Nederland AI-koploper kunnen worden.

Post 14: At TechDays 2025, we’re bringing two workshops you can’t miss if you’re building (or want to build) with conversational AI and multi-agent systems. 

⚙️ Workshop 1: 
Learn how to secure conversational AI by designing, implementing, and testing guardrails that block malicious content from chatbot applications. Approaches will include task-specific ML models, LLM-based solutions, and rule-based methods. 

⚙️ Workshop 2: 
Using AGNTCY’s CoffeeAgntcy reference implementation, you’ll build and run your own multi-agent system simulating a coffee supply chain. You’ll see a supervisor agent communicate with multiple agents via the SLIM protocol and even extend the setup by adding your own servers or components. 

The workshops will be led by ML6 | Your partner in AI (powered by Cisco) — who will share real lessons from the field and guide you through the hands-on labs. Cas Coopman, Cristóbal Sendín, Thomas Vrancken, Tom Istaz, Boaz Kwakkel

Post 15: Nine months ago, we kicked off an exciting journey with the Emergency Department (ED) of Erasmus MC, the winner of the National AI Challenge 2024. Together, we set out to tackle a critical issue: unpredictable patient inflow and limited treatment capacity.

Our question: How can AI help doctors and nurses better anticipate and manage patient inflow?

Over the course of the project:
🩺 Shadowed 5 shifts in the ED (in full nurse uniforms!) and interviewed dozens of doctors and nurses.
 🤝 Collaborated across 8 departments to combine medical, organizational, and technical expertise.
 📚 Hosted learning sessions to share insights and build AI knowledge inside the hospital.

The result? An AI-powered dashboard that:
✅ Predicts expected patient inflow in real time
✅ Provides long-term trends for smarter scheduling
✅ Helps staff shift from reacting to crises to managing resources proactively

For us, this project was about much more than building technology. It was about collaboration, empathy, and learning how AI can truly support healthcare professionals where it matters most: in patient care.

A big thank you to everyone at Erasmus MC and all the teams involved in making this happen. 👏 

#AI #NationalAIChallenge #KickstartAI

Post 16: 🔥 Our latest podcast matched summer temperatures as we moved from ashes to algorithms and how AI is resurrecting ancient 2,000-year-old texts.

How do you virtually unroll a scroll that would crumble to dust if opened? And how do you train a model to spot invisible, carbon-based ink?

🎙️ In our new episode, Evertjan Peer, Cascha van Wanrooij, and Ioanna Lykiardopoulou unpack the Vesuvius Challenge — an ongoing competition where machine learning, computer vision, and X-ray tomography come together to read scrolls buried in 79 AD after Mount Vesuvius’ eruption.

They break down how the winning team of the Grand Prize 2023 improved  virtual unrolling and ink detection, borrowing techniques from video models to revive the words of the philosopher Philodemus of Gadara, once thought lost.

📖 It’s a story of history meeting AI, with breakthroughs that could transform how we recover fragile texts across the world!

Post 12: 🚨 Our new technical blog is live!
 We explore how we used network analysis and LLMs to map the Netherlands’ threat landscape.

As part of our contribution to DenkWerk’s new report “Resilience by Design,” our ML engineer Cascha van Wanrooij built a node analysis tool to uncover how national threats interconnect, amplify each other, and where intervention can have the greatest impact.

🔹 Creating an interactive graph for exploring systemic threats
🔹 Tacking the statistical challenge of rare events

Post 13: 💡 To unlock AI’s potential, we have to stop treating it like another IT upgrade. We have to rethink how we work and operate.

As Sophia Claire Zitman pointed out at TNW Conference, AI is too big to comprehend and too different in nature from what we’re used to. And that is why so many organizations struggle to turn potential into impact.

They’re blocked by misaligned incentives. They keep approaching AI as a technology rollout — when in reality, it’s a shift in mindset. It’s not about hiring more developers or running more pilots. Nor about using traditional metrics to measure success.

👉 It’s about the people who are ready to reimagine what’s possible — and act on it. That’s exactly what we do at KickstartAI.

#AI #AIadoptionNetherlands #KickstartAI

Post 14: 🧠 The smartest thing humans do? Ignore most of the data.

In our latest podcast, Evertjan Peer, Cascha van Wanrooij, and Paul Kuyer explore what sets human intelligence apart from AI — and why that difference matters in practice.

AI learns by consuming massive amounts of data. But humans evolved to ignore most information, and act on the small fraction that matters the most.

The contrast goes beyond how we process information. It also shapes what we prioritize, remember, and understand, which influences how we behave. The AI systems we’re currently using have different foundations:
🔎  Current AI architectures don't have an integrated, persistent (and autobiographical) long-term memory
⚙️ Current models operate without intent or self-awareness
📉 But they’re still being used to generate decisions and influence at scale

So what kind of guardrails do we need when deploying systems that lack the constraints and instincts that shaped human cognition?

#TheKickstartAIPodcast #AI #ArtificialIntelligence

h DenkWerk on their new report examining how the Netherlands can better prepare for an increasingly interconnected threat landscape.

🔎 The challenge: 
Traditional threat analysis treats risks in isolation. But today, threats are deeply intertwined. A cyberattack can disrupt supply chains. Climate events can ripple into economic shocks. As COVID showed, health crises can spark nationwide consequences.

💡 Our contribution: 
Thanks to the work of our ML Engineer Cascha van Wanrooij, we developed a node analysis tool that maps how threats connect and amplify each other.

We used LLMs to extract causal relationships from thousands of government and research documents — creating a semantic network of interlinked national threats. The result: an interactive graph that helps policymakers explore these complex threat relationships visually, moving beyond traditional "probability × impact" models to include interconnectedness as a crucial third dimension. Post 8: Yesterday at TNW Conference, our very own Sophia Claire Zitman, addressed why AI adoption is lagging despite its €85bn potential for the Dutch economy. The problem isn't the tech or talent; it's that AI is too big to fully grasp.

As Sophia said: “AI isn’t digital transformation. It's not about automating things. It is about rewriting, reshaping, and rethinking the way you work.” 

To realize its potential, we must go beyond traditional metrics like OKRs and KPIs. They simply don’t work. The key is finding the entrepreneurial spirits and change-makers within your organizations — and enabling them to break the mould.  

⚙️ In our projects, this approach has led to real results:
🥗 KLM Royal Dutch Airlines cut food waste by 65%
📦 bol improved parcel delivery efficiency by 6%
🚉 Nederlandse Spoorwegen train stations became safer

Let's stop forcing AI into old frameworks — and embrace the change it brings. 

#KickstartAI #TNWConference2025 #AIadoptionNetherlands

Post 7: Great news! 🚀 We collaborated with DenkWerk on their new report examining how the Netherlands can better prepare for an increasingly interconnected threat landscape.

🔎 The challenge: 
Traditional threat analysis treats risks in isolation. But today, threats are deeply intertwined. A cyberattack can disrupt supply chains. Climate events can ripple into economic shocks. As COVID showed, health crises can spark nationwide consequences.

💡 Our contribution: 
Thanks to the work of our ML Engineer Cascha van Wanrooij, we developed a node analysis tool that maps how threats connect and amplify each other.

We used LLMs to extract causal relationships from thousands of government and research documents — creating a semantic network of interlinked national threats. The result: an interactive graph that helps policymakers explore these complex threat relationships visually, moving beyond traditional "probability × impact" models to include interconnectedness as a crucial third dimension. (Link to our tool: https://lnkd.in/dqej7mDQ)

👉 Read the full report here: https://lnkd.in/d7ABqq7f

#ResilienceByDesign #AIforGood #KickstartAI

Post 3: At the Dutch AI Congress this week, we had the opportunity to share key insights on how to develop and implement AI solutions in healthcare organizations.

For the past seven months, we’ve been working closely with Erasmus MC to predict patient inflow at the emergency department — a step toward improving efficiency, capacity management, and staffing.

Our Tech Lead, Evertjan P., took the stage to talk about what it really takes to apply AI in hospital operational processes. Here are three of the most important lessons we've learned so far:

💡 Thoroughly understand and define the problem you’re aiming to solve:
This is key to creating a specific goal and a concrete plan.

💡 Assess feasibility: 
What data sources are available, and what level of performance can an AI model achieve based on these? How can we use these AI-driven insights in day-to-day practice?

💡 Determine the processes for tech integration and user adoption:
Ask: What’s the IT infrastructure like? How will the AI tool be presented to end-users? How should it be used in practice?

We’re proud to be part of the AI movement in the Netherlands — and excited for what’s next. 🧡 

A big shoutout to the AI Coalitie 4 Nederland | AIC4NL for organizing this inspiring congress!

#AI #HealthcareAI #HealthcareInnovation

Post 6: 🚀 We’re excited to kick off our project with Politie Nederland and start working on an AI tool to support the fight against environmental crime. 

While environmental crime is the third largest criminal activity in the world, the respective laws are complex and environmental criminals know how to cover their tracks. But in doing so, they’re prone to violating other laws. 

💡 That’s why the idea is to develop a tool that can search for all relevant legislation beyond the scope of environmental law, potentially uncovering criminal offences that are easier to prove. 

Think of Al Capone, who ultimately didn’t get convicted for the many murders tied to his name, but for something entirely different: tax evasion. 

The Police was one of the winners of the National AI Challenge 2025 we organized together with AI Coalitie 4 Nederland | AIC4NL. As part of the prize, our team of ML engineers will help bring their AI idea to life, from development to adoption. 

At KickstartAI, we believe AI is a force for good. We look forward to continue working with the Police on a project with such far-reaching societal impact. 

⚡ Stay tuned! 

#AIforGood #AI #AISocialImpact

Post 4: 🇪🇺 Can Europe really achieve its AI ambitions?

If we look at the numbers, the EU is falling behind the US and China — in infrastructure, foundation models, investment, startups, and adoption.

And now, with the first obligations of the AI Act for general-purpose AI models officially in effect, the stakes are even higher. Across the tech sector, there’s growing concern that instead of accelerating innovation, these measures might be slowing it down.

In our latest podcast, Nils Beers and Ioanna Lykiardopoulou explore the real-world impact

belief: Identifying AI use cases in large organisations should be democratised, with challenges sourced from across the business and vetted systematically.
source_quote:
port of Large Language Models (LLMs). “They extract relevant information from those documents and transfer them into a system, where our colleagues can verify that we followed all of the compliance steps, and that we are able to verify and audit that we have cited that information from our client.”

Harding notes that AI has been instrumental in streamlining document processing, reducing an average time of six hours to a few seconds.


>>> Centralizing knowledge <<<

Harding’s team is currently experimenting with LLMs that can help centralize knowledge for internal use. The primary goal is to provide back-office experts with a single, up-to-date, accurate source of information.

“We want to make our internal content more accessible and easier to interpret, so that a service agent can quickly and concisely synthesize relevant information and respond more effectively,” Harding says. She believes that this will also have a significant impact in client service and customer engagement, as it will be possible to cross-reference information related to the specific conditions of a particular customer.

“We’re starting small and I do anticipate challenges around hallucinations,” she says. The team keeps testing the model and challenges it with wrong answers or difficult questions to evaluate its performance.

“So far, it hasn’t hallucinated, which makes us more confident that we’re following the right approach.”


--- Behind the curtain ---


>>> Identifying potential use cases <<<

“Our philosophy is to democratize the inclusion of different challenges from across the wholesale banking department,” Harding says. This means that any employee can submit particular issues or challenges they’re experiencing. These submissions are then flagged against key business objectives.

At this point, a dedicated team called “Discovery Squad” takes over. The squad reviews the highest-priority challenges through a defined vetting process. They define the problem, assess its technical feasibility and business viability, and secure risk approval. Then follows the proof-of-concept development process.

“One of the critical criteria we put in place when making the high-priority problems list is whether we can have access to healthy and clean data,” Harding says. “If we don’t, but still believe we want to work on a particular use case, we need to ensure that, if there is a data gap, those attributes aren’t something that can throw the algorithm off, or bias our results.”


>>> Measuring the success of AI <<<

To evaluate the success of AI solutions, ING uses three main metrics, starting with  quantitative metrics. These include the projected cost savings and efficiency gains, AI’s expected accuracy in solving the problem, and the capital investment needed.

“With AI, as with any breakthrough innovation, it’s more challenging to balance your evaluation scorecard,” Harding says. “We need to make a high upfront capital investment in order to achieve cost and efficiency savings.”

On the qualitative side is employee feedback. “This will also include client feedback when we get to the stage t

belief: Shadowing staff and observing workflows are essential practices for grounding AI projects in reality and surfacing true bottlenecks.
source_quote:
or break large-scale AI initiatives. And these aren’t just “nice to have.” They’re essential to making sure a solution is sustainable, valuable, and actually used.

We wanted to know:

What is the real operational problem here, beyond the initial idea?

How does the current process work, and where are the true bottlenecks?

How might it change the day-to-day work of staff on the ground?

And who will ultimately own, use, and maintain the solution?

By asking these questions, we ensure that the solution:

Fits with the organization’s strategy and priorities.

Aligns with how people really work, so it’s more likely to be accepted.

Aligns with how people really work, so it’s more likely to be accepted.

Has a clear owner and a path for long-term maintenance.

Has a clear owner and a path for long-term maintenance.

💡TIP: Our approach goes beyond just asking questions in meetings. We shadow staff, observe their workflows, and sometimes even step into the work ourselves. This hands-on approach grounds our understanding in reality, validates assumptions, and surfaces the things that really matter.


--- Lesson 1: Invest time in defining the business problem ---

When Erasmus MC first came to us, the question on the table was: how can we reduce the moments the ER goes on “red” — reaching maximum capacity and unable to safely accept new patients?

It was a real challenge, but also extremely broad. “Going red” could be triggered by many different things, such as patient inflow, discharge bottlenecks, room availability, or staffing shortages.

That’s why we began with a dedicated 3-4 month “Use Case Identifier” phase before any intensive model development began. The goal wasn’t to write code, but to understand the problem deeply from the hospital’s perspective.

Stakeholder interviews

Talking to everyone involved — from frontline staff to management — surfaced one key insight: the central role of the managing nurse. This person is the operational hub of the ER, deciding which nurse takes which patient, when to redirect patients elsewhere in the hospital, and how to keep flow moving.

Walking the floor

We shadowed staff and asked them to explain their work “as if to a new colleague,” making sure nothing obvious was left unsaid.  We saw firsthand how big of a task it  was for managing nurses to make capacity decisions with incomplete information. They were the ones truly “feeling the pain.” Mapping the process

Post 7: We are proud to share that our CEO, Erick Webbe, will be taking part in a roundtable discussion at the Dutch Parliament (Tweede Kamer der Staten-Generaal) on 26 March about optimizing AI opportunities for the Netherlands.

At KickstartAI, our position is clear: The Netherlands 🇳🇱  has many prerequisites in place – talent, infrastructure, and a strong business climate – but we are losing momentum to low AI implementation capacity and structures that weren't built for the speed of this technology. As a result, we're not realizing the full potential of AI and its benefits for society.

Erick will be advocating for a national shift from talk to action. To do that, we need:
✅ Space for execution and a mandate for practical experimentation. 
✅ Moving past existing structures to prioritize performance and scale. 
✅ Bridging the impact gap by tackling today’s most complex challenges

We know this is possible through our own projects – from reducing food waste with Ahold Delhaize, to optimizing logistics with Ampère, and improving financial health with ING.

As AI becomes a “system technology”– much like electricity or the internet – what does deliberate application look like and what kind of challenges do organizations need to solve?

Post 15: 📖 In our latest article, we take a closer look at the limits of benchmark-driven AI research – and why today’s scores often tell us less than we think.

We spoke with Maarten C. Stol, Chief Scientific Officer at Braincreators, about how benchmarks like ImageNet shaped modern AI, but also narrowed what we consider progress.

We dive into: 
🔸 How benchmark saturation changes what accuracy scores actually represent
🔸 Why what models get wrong is more revealing than what they get right
🔸 Shifting evaluation from performance numbers to the quality and nature of error cases

👇 Read the full article below and share your thoughts in the comments!

Post 16: Yesterday, ING and KickstartAI - together with our partners Ahold Delhaize, KLM Royal Dutch Airlines and Nederlandse Spoorwegen - hosted the AI team of the Ministry of Economic Affairs for a working visit focused on practical AI application and adoption.

🇳🇱 KickstartAI is built on the belief that accelerating the Dutch economy through AI requires shared learning and action. During the session, our founding partners shared practical insights from the AI projects we’re carrying out together: 
🔸 What it really takes to move from idea to implementation and adoption
🔸  How to set clear goals, and how to embed AI into the core of their organisations - not as side projects, but as drivers of impact.

We discussed how organisations can strengthen their AI adoption by learning through doing, by looking at AI not only as a technology but also in terms of organisational change and broader impact, and by integrating it deeply into customer-facing and operational processes. 

We also explored how the government can help lower barriers and accelerate this transition.

⚡ Our priority at KickstartAI is clear: the urgency of AI adoption is real, and action is needed now for the Netherlands to stay competitive, economically resilient, and technologically independent.

Thanks to the Ministry for the open and constructive exchange, and to our partners for sharing their experiences so openly. The work continues!

#AI #AINetherlands #AIAdoption

Post 14: 🔥 What do AI, a volcano, and ancient texts have in common?

In our latest podcast, Evertjan Peer, Cascha van Wanrooij, and Ioanna Lykiardopoulou break down the Vesuvius Challenge — an ongoing competition that combines ML, computer vision, and X-ray tomography to read 2,000-year-old scrolls turned into charcoal by Vesuvius’ eruption in 79AD.

They discuss how the winning team of the Grand Prize 2023 improved virtual unrolling and ink detection, borrowing techniques from video models to revive lost words of the philosopher Philodemus of Gadara. 

🎧 History meets AI! Tune in to the full discussion: https://lnkd.in/eWcaJkJx

#TheKickstartAIpodcast #VesuviusChallenge #AI

Post 15: Nine months ago, we kicked off an exciting journey with the Emergency Department (ED) of Erasmus MC, the winner of the National AI Challenge 2024. Together, we set out to tackle a critical issue: unpredictable patient inflow and limited treatment capacity.

Our question: How can AI help doctors and nurses better anticipate and manage patient inflow?

Over the course of the project:
🩺 Shadowed 5 shifts in the ED (in full nurse uniforms!) and interviewed dozens of doctors and nurses.
 🤝 Collaborated across 8 departments to combine medical, organizational, and technical expertise.
 📚 Hosted learning sessions to share insights and build AI knowledge inside the hospital.

The result? An AI-powered dashboard that:
✅ Predicts expected patient inflow in real time
✅ Provides long-term trends for smarter scheduling
✅ Helps staff shift from reacting to crises to managing resources proactively

For us, this project was about much more than building technology. It was about collaboration, empathy, and learning how AI can truly support healthcare professionals where it matters most: in patient care.

A big thank you to everyone at Erasmus MC and all the teams involved in making this happen. 👏 

#AI #NationalAIChallenge #KickstartAI

Post 4: 🇪🇺 Can Europe really achieve its AI ambitions?

If we look at the numbers, the EU is falling behind the US and China — in infrastructure, foundation models, investment, startups, and adoption.

And now, with the first obligations of the AI Act for general-purpose AI models officially in effect, the stakes are even higher. Across the tech sector, there’s growing concern that instead of accelerating innovation, these measures might be slowing it down.

In our latest podcast, Nils Beers and Ioanna Lykiardopoulou explore the real-world impact of the EU’s AI Act — and what it could mean for the future of AI in Europe.

🎧 Listen to the full discussion here: https://lnkd.in/eMXWnPE6
#AI #AIEurope #EUAIAct

Post 16: At yesterday’s KickstartAI x distil labs workshop, we rolled up our sleeves to explore how fine-tuning SLMs can unlock powerful and cost-effective RAG systems.

In this episode, our hosts Evertjan Peer and Cascha V. sit down with Johan de Vries, Tech Lead Battery Optimization at Dexter Energy. Together, they explore how data and AI are used to manage Europe’s increasingly complex energy grid. 

We cover: 

📊 High-resolution forecasting:
 How Dexter uses weather data to predict renewable energy generation — and why precision matters as volatility grows.

 More renewables, more electric vehicles, and more prosumers mean more complexity — and smarter solutions.
💥 Plus: how France helped mitigate the Spain/Portugal outage — and more! 

This one is a deep dive into the future of energy, data, and AI. 🔌

Post 16: We took Nvidia’s Cosmos model for a spin and created a realistic driving simulation in Amsterdam 🚘 

Advancements in machine learning show that even relatively small models (like the 7B-parameter Cosmos) can generate diverse, physically plausible synthetic data, scenarios, and even entire worlds on demand.

This capability enables developers to test AI systems across billions of virtual kilometers and countless scenarios — leading to safer, more capable AI for real-world applications like driving.

Post 1: 🗣️ Applications for this year’s National AI Challenge are open!

Together with AI Coalitie 4 Nederland | AIC4NL, we’re calling on organizations looking to use AI to solve some of the world’s most pressing societal challenges. This is your chance to create solutions that drive real change and improve people’s lives!

Following a successful 2024 edition, this year, we’ll select two winners and work with them on two separate projects.

💡 Who can apply? 
Private, public, or social organisations that are (or interested in becoming) members of AIC4NL.

Selection criteria: 
➡️ Relevance and urgency of the problem
➡️ Available support by your organisation
➡️ Feasibility
➡️ Impact and scalability
➡️ Willingness to share the results

Why join? 
✅ Develop a working AI solution for your organization together with experts
✅ Make social impact 
✅ Gain access to technical expertise, project support, and a powerful network
✅ Increase the visibility of your organization as an AI pioneer 
✅ Become part of an inspiring ecosystem where collaboration is key 

🗓️ Deadline for applications: July 25, 2025

Interested in more information or the application form? Follow the link below: https://lnkd.in/etNzMRHD

Post 3: At the Dutch AI Congress this week, we had the opportunity to share key insights on how to develop and implement AI solutions in healthcare organizations.

For the past seven months, we’ve been working closely with Erasmus MC to predict patient inflow at the emergency department — a step toward improving efficiency, capacity management, and staffing.

Our Tech Lead, Evertjan P., took the stage to talk about what it really takes to apply AI in hospital operational processes. Here are three of the most important lessons we've learned so far:

💡 Thoroughly understand and define the problem you’re aiming to solve:
This is key to creating a specific goal and a concrete plan.

💡 Assess feasibility: 
What data sources are available, and what level of performance can an AI model achieve based on these? How can we use these AI-driven insights in day-to-day practice?

💡 Determine the processes for tech integration and user adoption:
Ask: What’s the IT infrastructure like? How will the AI tool be presented to end-users? How should it be used in practice?

We’re proud to be part of the AI movement in the Netherlands — and excited for what’s next. 🧡 

A big shoutout to the AI Coalitie 4 Nederland | AIC4NL for organizing this inspiring congress!

#AI #HealthcareAI #HealthcareInnovation

Post 4: Very proud to announce that we once again joined forces with AI Coalitie 4 Nederland | AIC4NL to accelerate AI adoption in the Netherlands. Frans Muller, CEO of Ahold Delhaize, one of the founding partner companies of KickstartAI, today unveiled the second edition of the National AI Challenge, the flagship initiative of our renewed collaboration. 

The National AI Challenge was first introduced by KickstartAI and AI Coalition 4 NL in 2024, aiming to solve  concrete challenges in businesses and social organizations with artificial intelligence. The winner of the first edition, Erasmus MC, has worked with KickstartAI over the past year to improve efficiency and quality of care in the Emergency Department using AI technology.

This year’s National AI Challenge will start at the end of April and will select not one, but two use cases.

Read more about our collaboration in our press release https://lnkd.in/d-MnC-Vc  and stay tuned for more details about how to participate in the Challenge!
