belief: AI adoption requires leaders to think beyond local optimisations and redesign entire processes where appropriate, not just speed up existing steps.
source_quote:
asks of people.

“AI forces employees to work more critically,” says Dusée. “You have to learn not only how to use it, but also how to question it. Where are the limits? When do you trust the output, and when do you not? What thresholds do you apply? That requires expertise, not blind trust.”

He adds: “The pace of change is high. Two years ago, we were talking about chatbots; now we are already talking about agentic AI and conversational AI. That is why you should not only train employees on today’s tools, but above all teach them how to keep adapting, spot opportunities, and help steer AI adoption.”

That is why ING, he says, is deliberately investing in training, with programs for both employees and leaders. Not because everyone needs to become a technical specialist, but because AI does not work if it remains a toy for a small group of experts.

“Often, someone spots an opportunity within their own scope, for example when creating a report. That is a good start, but for leaders the real work only begins after that: can you apply such a solution more broadly? Not for one report, but for all reports, or perhaps even for the entire process? That requires choices. You cannot do everything at once. Sometimes you have to say very explicitly: these are the six use cases we will focus on this year, and we will leave those other twenty aside for now.”

Continuing to challenge and question yourself is also important when it comes to changing processes through AI, he emphasizes.

“The first reflex is often: how can I add AI while keeping everything else the same? But the more interesting question is: should this process actually be fundamentally redesigned?”

At ING, he sees this, for example, in the manual processes used to collect data and make it available for models. The question is not only which step can be made faster, but whether the entire chain should be redesigned.

“Only then does AI become a strategic question,” he says. “Not because executives are operating the technology themselves, but because they determine what gets priority, how it is embedded and who is responsible for it.”

That, according to Dusée, is where things often go wrong in practice. Not with the technology, but with the organization.

“Many pilots fail because organizations think about day two too late. Teams build something in a safe environment and it works. But only then does the real question arise: where does this land in the organization? In which process? Which team becomes the owner? How do you maintain it? How does it connect to existing systems? If you only start asking those questions after the pilot, you get friction. Other teams have not been involved, the processes do not align, or there is simply no capacity to roll it out. Then something remains stuck in a kind of innovation limbo.”

To prevent this, ING has set up a clear structure for AI: a central analytics team that oversees the portfolio, while concrete ideas can still come from the shop floor.

“That central direction is essential to prevent teams from reinventing the wheel in their own small corner. The aim is to work in an ‘agnostic’ way: a solution that works for one type

belief: Identifying AI use cases should be a collaborative process that combines AI expertise with deep, insider knowledge of business processes and pain points.
source_quote:
firm, where he worked as a senior software engineer and, later on, as tech lead and CTO.

Marinus’ interest in digital technology also started early on in his life. He built his first website when he was around 12 years old, and created his first company — in web hosting — when he was 16. He has since founded multiple startups and is specialized in digital strategy and product management and development.

“What we’ve both seen in our past,” Alexander says, “is that 90% of initiatives in software and digital product development have failed. The reason why has mostly to do with the fact that companies don’t think about the end customer.”

“And we see that a lot of companies currently trying to use AI are making the same kind of mistake. They focus too much on small efficiency gains. They forget the huge impact it will have on how work is performed and what is considered valuable by customers in the future.”

This is what the duo has set out to do differently with Algemi. The startup both helps companies identify AI use cases and builds custom AI and software solutions. Currently Algemi consists of a core team of four, supported by a vetted freelance pool.


--- Identifying AI use cases ---

According to Marinus, Algemi’s approach to identifying where AI can bring value relies on combining knowledge and effort.

“From our side, we know a lot about AI and what it can do. We also have a pretty broad understanding of how different types of businesses work,” he explains.

However, the most important factor, he says, is gaining a deep understanding of the business from the inside, which can only be done in close collaboration with Algemi’s clients. “They know their processes. They know their customers. They know the pain points and what they envision for the future.”

To guide their work, Marinus and Alexander use a framework that divides AI use cases in three parts. The first is efficiency gains — using AI to optimize existing operations. The second part is growth, looking at previously unfeasible products or services that AI can enable. The third part focuses on where AI itself may put a business at risk.

Once the team identifies the potential use cases, they pick the ones that can bring value in the short term. “We like to focus first on the low-hanging fruit,” Alexander says. “This provides customers with an easy first experience with AI. And with AI developing so quickly, what is now difficult to solve might be easy in the next few months.”

The team then builds a proof of concept to test whether their AI solution is feasible. The next phase is developing a Minimum Viable Product (MVP), involving the end users as much as possible throughout the process.

“We let them try it out, collaborate closely, and learn what tools they already use and how the solution can fit into their existing workflow,” Alexander explains. “A tool is used most often and effectively when it is easy to apply and fits well within your normal workflow.”

Following the MVP’s launch, the duo focuses on fine-tuning and improving based on user feedback.


--- Putting LLMs to work ---

When building AI solutions, Algemi leverages existing LLMs, s

belief: Non‑technical staff in AI organisations should be actively upskilled through structured learning initiatives to understand the technology around them.
source_quote:
g problems — not on operations and administration. My role is to create a really strong base that enables everyone to do their best work and achieve their goals. I'm there to support them in getting where they need to go.


>>> What drives you in your work? <<<

Building KickstartAI’s foundation behind the scenes is definitely what drives me. Empowering others also motivates me. Seeing the team thrive and knowing that my support helps my colleagues is very important to me.

Part of this is creating the right office environment and culture, but also making sure we hire the right people who get along and complement each other.


>>> What interested you about working in AI? <<<

To be honest, I ended up in AI by accident! As I like the tech industry in general, I thought this would be an incredible experience and an opportunity to stay in a technical space and work with technical people. I find that my personality matches well with a more introverted technical team. We get along really well!


>>> What do you enjoy most about working at KickstartAI? <<<

I love that my role is so varied — from HR to finance — so every day is different. It keeps things interesting!

I also value our open and transparent culture. Everyone's friendly with each other, and I enjoy going to the office because it's always fun. We laugh a lot and it's never boring.

We used to organize "lunch and learn" sessions with deep dives for the technical team and more general sessions for our non-technical people who wanted to better understand AI. Last year, we even did a "100 Days of Coding" challenge with the group of non-techies. Every week, we'd sit together for an hour and try coding ourselves to really understand what was happening around us. That was a lot of fun!


>>> What AI tools do you use most in your daily work? <<<

Google Gemini helps me the most, especially when working with contracts. I can quickly search paragraphs without reading through 20-30 pages. I use AI assistants also when writing emails to be more efficient and find the right tone of voice.

I've also started using Venice.ai. It's a collection of models where you can choose between Deep Seek and Llama. I prefer it over ChatGPT now.


>>> What advice would you give to people who want to work in AI or at KickstartAI? <<<

Just be yourself! And be passionate about tech and AI — that's what makes someone a great fit in our team and culture. Also, if you’re looking for diverse projects, we are the place to be — you won’t get bored here. We're actually planning to double our people by the end of the year if we can hire and recruit the technical engineers and project managers we need. The open vacancies are already on our website . We’re definitely expanding the dream team!


>>> Fun fact about yourself? <<<

My way to show I care is feeding people with sweets. So be aware, our office is packed with candy!

belief: Data‑for‑good projects in nonprofits cultivate end‑to‑end thinking, generalist skills, and cost‑impact awareness that transfer back into commercial roles.
source_quote:
, and bureaucracy.

To support this, the CorrelAid team built a proper database, where applications from welcome groups and newcomers could be stored and later used for algorithmic matching. “Good matches” could be defined in many ways, but geographical proximity was a clear factor, Anastasiia explains.

“I contributed to the foundational part of data engineering, setting up the infrastructure,” she explains. “The nonprofit had previously relied on surveys and manual input in different formats, with no existing databases.”


--- Battling homelessness ---

Another project , which Anastasiia participated in, focused on helping people who were economically homeless — those unable to afford housing — by placing them in spare hotel rooms.

Hotels could offer vacancies free of charge, giving people a temporary place to stay, sometimes just long enough to prepare for a job interview or take first steps toward employment.

The project was less about advanced data science and more about data management and storytelling, Anastasiia explains, to support the goals of CorrelAid’s partner organization.

“The first goal was operational efficiency: much of their work was done manually, and without a single source of ‘truth’ they couldn’t effectively identify whether hotel rooms were available or not.” The second goal, she adds, was to raise funding. “They wanted to communicate the project’s positive impact in numbers and create a compelling visualization for donors.”


--- How data for good projects can change a data scientist’s perspective ---

“Working on data for good projects, especially as part of a nonprofit, has made me think more end-to-end in my approach,” Anastasiia notes, “seeing how my work connects to teams, end products, and the wider context.”

She adds that with nonprofits usually being financially strained, their people wear many hats, are multidisciplinary and multifunctional. “You gradually learn the same type of attitude. It’s likely that in your day job, you’re tied to a particular function or specialization — but in a nonprofit you learn how to be a generalist. We often hear volunteers say that the experience gave them the confidence to take more initiative at work and step outside their comfort zones.”

Volunteering at CorrelAid has also shifted how Anastasiia thinks about budgets. “As techies, we love shiny new things, but now I try to measure cost vs. impact, even in the commercial setting of my company, where funding is less of a problem.” She’s also more cautious in how she communicates at work, making sure to back her claims with data — a habit shaped by the transparency demands of nonprofit projects.

One of Anastasiia’s dreams is to make data for good volunteering more mainstream. The hurdle she sees is that, often, such projects seem less exciting from a technical perspective.

“A lot of us are technology geeks. We like to play with LLMs. We like to play with fast computes and new dashboards. But nonprofits are usually less technologically mature organizations, which may need help with setting up the data infrastructure, or creating their first database.”

belief: Finding product‑market fit in AI startups is an organisation‑wide, iterative struggle that shapes structure, talent, and go‑to‑market choices.
source_quote:
emini and focus on the data processing pipelines rather than the end-user interface.

Lennard explains that the startup’s core technology is the workflow engine — primarily built with JavaScript and powered by Natural Language Processing (NLP).  Underneath this engine, there are multiple building blocks, from various AI models to vector databases like Weviate.

In essence, Lleverage offers “describe-and-build” AI workflow solutions. This is what it calls “vibe automation.”

Such an architecture doesn’t come without its challenges, according to Lennard. The complexity and volume of data processing in user-defined pipelines create significant technical demands. Behind the scenes, Lleverage has to ensure data integrity, handle retries for external API failures, and manage the non-deterministic nature of AI outputs — all while keeping the experience smooth for the user.

But the most complex problem Lleverage has faced so far isn’t a technical one.


=== The greatest early-stage challenge: finding product-market fit ===

“The biggest challenge for a company in our stage is finding the right product-market fit,” Lennard says. “It’s an iffy concept.”

“When do you feel that your product metrics are giving you the signals that your positioning is right for you? Or when do you think that there’s not enough real traction and you need to keep changing and do another iteration on the product or the business proposition?”

“It’s a constant struggle,” he adds.

Lennard also stresses that the search for product-market fit influences every part of the business: from the business model and the pricing strategy to the type of talent needed and the overall go-to-market motion.

“When transitioning to a sales-led enterprise play, for example, you need to think of a different organizational structure, with a focus on customer success, solutions engineers, and dedicated account executives,” he says.

He compares this to a product-led growth strategy, where the focus shifts towards growth marketing and community management.

Lleverage itself is now in-between the sales-led and the product-led growth motion.

“We’re consciously not making a decision right now,” Lennard explains. “But we’re going to make one in the following months. We can’t maintain this position for a long time, because it defocuses a small company like us.”

Besides market fit, Lennard mentions another challenge for early-stage companies: funding. The startup raised €3mn earlier this month, bringing its total investment to €5mn in less than a year. It has managed to avoid the pitfalls of the funding rollercoaster thanks to its seasoned founders who have already built and scaled successful companies.

But for first-time founders, attracting capital — especially in the AI space — is no easy task, Lennard says. “You have to convince VCs about the defensibility of your idea and that you can actually outgrow your competition.”


=== Building a startup is no bed of roses ===

When asked about the biggest lesson he’s learned on his journey with Lleverage, Lennard doesn’t hesitate: “I had forgotten how painful it is to build a startup.”

He cautions prosp

o solve complex, real-world problems. Jesse's goal is to create solutions that people adopt and rely on, making impact part of the process.

Let's dive into his journey, what excites him about building AI, and how collaboration shapes his work.


>>> What’s your role at KickstartAI? <<<

I’m a machine learning engineer and I work on complex ML and AI projects for our partner companies. Basically, my job is about using the latest AI techniques to solve real-word problems, and that’s something I really enjoy.


>>> Can you share your journey into AI? <<<

Ever since I was a kid, I’ve liked doing things efficiently, for example by optimizing boring tasks so they take as little time as possible. That made math my favorite subject in school, which eventually led me to study industrial engineering. During my studies, I was introduced to data science and later pursued a master’s in data science and AI.

I started my career as a data scientist, and from there, moving into AI and machine learning engineering was a natural step. It's all about solving problems smarter and faster in production.


>>> What’s something in our projects that genuinely excites you? <<<

I find it very exciting that we’re not building AI for the sake of it, but that we’re trying to make it work in complex, real-world settings. Innovation is easy; adoption is the hard part. It’s often overlooked, but that’s what I find most challenging and rewarding.

This is why I really appreciate our approach to projects : we start by identifying the problems where AI can make a difference, and then create a solution around that in collaboration with the people who are actually going to use it. So building something together across teams and companies is definitely a very interesting part for me.


>>> What do you enjoy about working at KickstartAI? <<<

Besides the focus on AI adoption, I really like that there’s room to innovate and experiment. We’re encouraged to stay up to date with the latest advancements, so there’s always this buzz in the office people asking, “Have you read this?” or “Have you seen that?”

I haven’t experienced this anywhere else, and it’s very cool that we get to transfer this knowledge to our projects. For example, a recent paper that came out largely applied the same methods and techniques we’re using in our project with NS on unattended object detection at train stations the project where I focus on. For me, that was a great validation that we’re working at the forefront of AI development


>>> What drives you in your work? <<<

What drives me the most is seeing the real-world impact of what I build. It’s not just about writing code or training a model it’s about seeing that work translate into something people actually use. === From idea to impact: lessons on how to implement AI in large organizations ===


--- The Erasmus MC case ---

By Sophia Zitman & Evertjan Peer

Many AI projects start with big ideas and impressive proofs-of-concept, but often fail before they create any real value inside a large organization. The barrier is rarely the technology. It’s making the leap from a pilot to a solution that fits the organization’s actual processes, strategy, and people.

At KickstartAI, our focus is on closing this implementation gap. A recent project with Erasmus MC — where we built a system to predict patient inflow — in their busy emergency department, offers clear lessons on what it really takes to move AI from idea to impact.


--- The initial spark vs the real need ---

The project began with a clear technical proposal: build a model that predicts patient number arrivals in the ER so staff could manage capacity better. On paper, it was a solid idea — technically sound and tied to an obvious need.

But experience has taught us that many AI projects stall when they start with technology first. A clever model alone rarely delivers lasting impact. That’s why, right from the beginning, we asked the kinds of questions that make or break large-scale AI initiatives. And these aren’t just “nice to have.” They’re essential to making sure a solution is sustainable, valuable, and actually used.

We wanted to know:

What is the real operational problem here, beyond the initial idea?

What is the real operational problem here, beyond the initial idea?

How does the current process work, and where are the true bottlenecks?

How does the current process work, and where are the true bottlenecks?

How does this challenge tie into the organization’s broader strategy and management priorities?

How does this challenge tie into the organization’s broader strategy and management priorities?

How might it change the day-to-day work of staff on the ground?

How might it change the day-to-day work of staff on the ground?

And who will ultimately own, use, and maintain the solution?

And who will ultimately own, use, and maintain the solution?

By asking these questions, we ensure that the solution:

Tackles actual bottlenecks, not assumptions.

Tackles actual bottlenecks, not assumptions.

Fits with the organization’s strategy and priorities.

Fits with the organization’s strategy and priorities.

Aligns with how people really work, so it’s more likely to be accepted.

Aligns with how people really work, so it’s more likely to be accepted.

Has a clear owner and a path for long-term maintenance.

Has a clear owner and a path for long-term maintenance.

Post 1: On Monday, KickstartAI joined Minister Vincent Karremans and a small group of industry leaders at the Ministry of Economic Affairs for a roundtable on AI adoption in the Netherlands.

🇳🇱 The urgency around labour productivity, our national earning capacity, and ensuring a positive societal impact  is undeniable – and it demands action now. If we want to stay competitive, we need to treat the Netherlands as a scale-up country for AI adoption – moving beyond high-level reflections to the reality of implementation in day-to-day operations.

For the past four years, KickstartAI and our founding partners have been working exactly there: in the core of organizations. Our experience shows that meaningful AI adoption is a steep learning curve. It means tackling real problems, embedding AI into core processes, and dealing with culture and governance – not just rolling out tools.

We believe large companies play a crucial role as the flywheel for Dutch AI innovation and practical adoption at scale. Our new CEO Erick Webbe indicated we are committed to staying the course, with the ambition to build on it nationally and deliver a tenfold increase in lasting impact: with big and small businesses. 

Looking at the new coalition agreement this is a shared goal. The focus now must be on turning it into practice. 

A sincere thank you to Vincent Karremans and Martijn Lucassen for the invitation and to all participants for the open and practical discussion Art de Blaauw, Hiek Van der Scheer, Juliette Goetzee, Saskia Mureau, Thomas Grosfeld, Jelle Prins!

Wie gelooft in de economie van de toekomst en digitaal koploper wil zijn, zal hier echt stappen in moeten zetten.

➡️ Van pilot naar implementatie; van klein beginnen naar opschalen en vooral in de praktijk doen. Bij KickstartAI herkennen we dat: al meer dan vijf jaar werken wij aan het versnellen van AI-adoptie. Wij kijken ernaar uit deze uitdaging samen met Kabinet en Kamer op te pakken.

Post 14: 🧠 The smartest thing humans do? Ignore most of the data.

In our latest podcast, Evertjan Peer, Cascha van Wanrooij, and Paul Kuyer explore what sets human intelligence apart from AI — and why that difference matters in practice.

AI learns by consuming massive amounts of data. But humans evolved to ignore most information, and act on the small fraction that matters the most.

The contrast goes beyond how we process information. It also shapes what we prioritize, remember, and understand, which influences how we behave. The AI systems we’re currently using have different foundations:
🔎  Current AI architectures don't have an integrated, persistent (and autobiographical) long-term memory
⚙️ Current models operate without intent or self-awareness

Post 15: 🧠 Is our brain a biological computer?

In our latest podcast, Evertjan Peer and Cascha van Wanrooij sit down with Paul Kuyer to unpack a core analogy that sits at the heart of the AGI debate: If the human brain is a biological computer, why can’t a digital computer eventually do all the same things and reach superhuman levels?

While the premise is plausible, the logic behind it also risks being self-reinforcing — assuming the thing it needs to prove. 

Yes, neural networks are powerful. They outperform us in specific tasks. But, at the moment at least, the technology lacks the essential traits that define human cognition: consciousness, wants, and desires.

💬 Are we anthropomorphizing models just because they speak our language? Or do the similarities between brains and machines run deeper than we’re comfortable admitting? And if they do, what kind of caution does this call for? 

👉 Listen to the full discussion here:https://lnkd.in/eFGHnCyE

#TheKickstartAIpodcast #AGI #AI

Post 16: AI holds a €85bn potential for the Dutch economy, but as Sophia Claire Zitman pointed out at TNW Conference, adoption is lagging.

It’s not about the technology, talent, or compute. It’s that AI is still stuck in the "potential" phase — too abstract, too big, too misaligned with how organizations actually work.

To unlock its impact, we have to stop treating AI like another IT upgrade. We have to rethink how we work and operate. We need to move from traditional metrics like OKRs and KPIs, to focusing on the people within organizations who are ready to reimagine what’s possible — and act on it.

In our projects, this approach has led to real results:
✈️  KLM Royal Dutch Airlines cut food waste by 65%
🛍️ bol improved parcel delivery efficiency by 6%
🚉 Nederlandse Spoorwegen train stations increasing safety 

👉 The potential is clear. The time to move from exploration to execution is now.

#KicstartAI #AIinNetherlands #AIadoption

Post 20: We’re thrilled to start our project with Leiden University Medical Center to create an AI tool that improves wound care and reduces nurse’s administrative burden!

🩹 What we’re building
In collaboration with LUMC’s Cairelab and the Nursing Information Officers (NIOs), we’re developing an AI model that can generate structured wound reports directly from photos

💡 Why this matters
Nurses dedicate valuable time to wound reporting — a manual, time-consuming process, which often takes place daily. Our goal is to let AI handle the routine steps: automatically detecting the wound area, classifying tissue types, and drafting a structured TIME-style report that nurses can quickly review and validate.

The impact:
 ✅ Less administrative work
 ✅ More consistent and objective wound reports
 ✅ Better communication and handovers
 ✅ More time for direct patient care

The goal is to have the model operational in the hospital within one year. After validation, the same approach could be used in more hospitals across the Netherlands. 

This project builds on LUMC’s success as one of the winners of the National AI Challenge 2025, organized together with AI Coalitie 4 Nederland | AIC4NL.  As part of the prize, our team of ML engineers will help bring this idea to life — from prototype to adoption.

At KickstartAI, we believe that AI is a force for good, and we’re proud to collaborate on innovations that have real impact on care, patients, and professionals 🤝 🇳🇱 

#AIinHealthcare #Healthcare #Innovation

--- The Dutch AI ecosystem ---

While the Netherlands is strong in leveraging existing AI models, it falls behind in foundational AI development, Alexander and Marinus note. They both advocate for further investment into building data centers in the country and advancing core AI capabilities.

“It’s something that’s going to be a strategic resource at some point,” Marinus says. “You don’t want half of your country to be relying on technology that’s hosted in a data center somewhere else. I also think it’d be a great idea to train a Dutch LLM.”

He nevertheless remains optimistic, seeing excitement across the sector and the creation of various AI communities, which could eventually lead to a single, all-encompassing one.

Marinus particularly appreciates the meetups of the KickstartAI community. “It’s a great opportunity to mingle, easily present what you’re doing, get to know what other people are working on, and keep up to date with developments.”

Alexander also sees value in exchanging ideas during the meetups, especially when it comes to creating awareness about the necessity of quickly implementing AI  within companies. “It’s interesting to see how other businesses are approaching this,” he says. “Sharing this knowledge is very valuable, and I think the market is large enough for all companies to survive. It’s more about winning together by solving the issue [of creating awareness for AI adoption] than about giving away our competing advantage.”

belief: Diverse national AI initiatives across Europe, even if fragmented, collectively generate data and experience that can be shared to avoid reinventing the wheel.

principles: they’re (almost exclusively) open-source, transparent, and linguistically inclusive. Yet, they also demonstrate differences in both development and vision.

Perhaps the most unique of the current national efforts is the Dutch GPT-NL project, according to Rijgersberg. Now nearing its completion, GPT-NL stands out with its commitment to using strictly opt-in data, released under permissive licenses, donated voluntarily, or provided through partnerships.

“This is an interesting experiment and one that Europe can learn from. But it’s also a very challenging strategy to execute, especially for a language like Dutch that already doesn’t have much available text data to train on,” Rijgersberg says.

That challenge doesn’t just affect training — it also shapes how open the model can be. Under its opt-in data approach, GPT-NL’s source code and openly licensed datasets will be made public, but the model weights and copyrighted material will remain behind research and professional licences.

Meanwhile, across Europe, countries are forming their own visions for AI. France, for instance, “is pouring a lot of money and attention into what I call ‘creating’ AI,” Rijgersberg says, “rejecting the idea that they have to rely on the US or China.” By contrast, the Netherlands’ focus is mainly on the correct and ethical use of generative AI applications, rather than on building the underlying GenAI models.

Still, Rijgersberg sees a critical benefit in this diversity of approaches: “All these country-level efforts gather data and experience that is shared all over Europe.”

“Countries don’t have to reinvent the wheel by themselves.”


--- Towards a pan-European open-source network ---

Performance-wise, European open-source LLMs do not yet match the best American and Chinese models, which Rijgersberg attributes to two primary reasons: the lack of computational power and the need to comply with the EU’s text and data mining rules.

“If you look at benchmarks, with all these smaller initiatives, we have surpassed the performance of Meta’s Llama 2. I would say that we are approximately about a year behind, which is still a million years in AI.”

But Rijgersberg remains optimistic, describing this as a “surmountable gap,” especially as the EU moves towards addressing the above two challenges. The AI Act now provides further clarity on data training requirements , while the bloc’s investment into 19 AI factories is expected to significantly increase computing power (including access to supercomputers), data resources, and technical expertise.

The AI factories are also signaling a path towards a pan-European infrastructure, where open-source models have a big role to play. “We’re starting to see some cooperation and open-source is the best form of sharing,” Rijgersberg says.

“It’s about putting everything in the open to allow people to learn from each other, come up with new ideas, and collaborate to create new pan-European initiatives.”

The open-source approach offers benefits that go beyond the strategic imperative of digital sovereignty. It a

Our work isn’t just about maximizing revenue or improving the usual KPIs, but also about helping people. I really like that. >>> How does your work impact the AI industry in the Netherlands? <<<

I think the most effective part of my work here is taking part in the challenges we do together with our partner companies. Imagine these challenges as structured, two-week hackathons, where members from all the different organizations come together to start developing an AI solution for a real-world problem.

You meet a lot of people and work intensively with them. Everyone contributes their experience and then you all leave with shared insights and knowledge. I think this has a positive impact on the Dutch AI ecosystem. The challenges might be limited to our partner companies, but we always share the learnings on our blog.


>>> What drives you in your work? <<<

A big driver for me is my curiosity about technology and the excitement of solving problems with it. Even before the big AI boom, I loved how software engineering let you build almost anything with code.

Now, with AI, you can create solutions faster, smarter, and on a completely new level. I’m working on things today that I wouldn’t have had the time or tools for just a few months ago. That’s very cool/motivating!


>>> What are your go-to AI tools? <<<

I mainly use coding assistants, such as Codex, Droid, and Gemini. I see these tools as productivity enablers, although you still have to be careful when using them. Sometimes they make mistakes, and you can easily end up spending more time fixing something than if you had done it yourself. So I try to be mindful about when to use them and when to rely on my own code.

I often switch between different coding assistants, depending on the task. I prefer those that let you change the underlying model without any reprogramming. Each model has its own strengths and weaknesses, and I like using the best tool for each job.

The classic advice is “learn the math.” But I’d like to offer another perspective: try to understand the processes behind the AI models. How are these models made and trained? Why do they produce certai

belief: Launching impactful AI products quickly requires small, trusted teams with 'maker time' and comfort with ambiguity rather than heavy structure.
source_quote:
on of hallucinations,” Iman adds. “We only want to share summaries that are safe and we prefer being on the conservative side.”


--- Building trust ---

Building trust into an AI product for healthcare is no easy task, but as Merlijn explains, Ditto benefits from being closely connected to the Dutch healthcare system .

Through both professional and personal ties, the team collaborates with different organizations and academic hospitals not only to present its solution, but also to engage in open dialogue about how to build it responsibly.

“Most of the doctors are very positive; others are more skeptical and have expressed privacy concerns about the recording feature,” Merlijn says. “What’s been working well for us in encouraging clinicians to test our app themselves. That has convinced many to start suggesting Ditto to their patients!”

Gaining user trust has to do a lot with clarity, he adds. “We try to be very clear on how to use the app, communicate why certain things wouldn’t work, and be transparent about privacy and data protection. We have consciously decided to not store medical data centrally, such that it is only stored on-device. Our users love that.”


--- Launching an AI product in less than a year ---

The startup launched its app in July this year, approximately seven months after the team started working on the product idea.

“It all starts with having the best people,” Merlijn says, adding the importance of creating “maker time” for the development team. “This means having the ability to make quick decisions instead of involving multiple people in endless discussions — and trusting each other to make the right call.”

Executing at speed is also about scoping things down and embracing ambiguity.

“In the beginning, I struggled with that because I was used to roles with extensive  structure and plans. But being comfortable with ambiguity allows you to be more agile: if you suddenly have a better idea, you can directly change course and move with that idea.”

Ditto has set big ambitions for the future. “There’s plenty of room to grow in the Netherlands next year, and I’m quite certain that there will eventually be international expansion,” Merlijn says. Product-wise, the startup will also focus on summary personalization and on extending the functionality of sharing with loved ones.

“This first year has definitely been a rollercoaster, with some hard lessons along the way,” he concludes. “But building a startup is the most fun I’ve ever had. Above all, working on something meaningful, something that helps people in such a tangible way, is what truly drives the team."

=== Employee Spotl

belief: Organisations should redesign processes into modular, asynchronous 'building blocks' so AI can be switched on or off without collapsing the system.
source_quote:
The broader lesson ---

This is also the lesson he wants to share with other organizations. “Never let AI become a strategy in itself. It should always be a means.”

In his own work, the starting point was very concrete: the slow start of complex environmental investigations. Not reforming the entire police organization, but addressing one clear bottleneck.

According to him, that’s where things often go wrong. Organizations start with the technology instead of the friction.

“The danger is that new technology ends up reinforcing the very problem you’re trying to solve”, he says.

He also sees how many organizations are still structured linearly, according to the model of the industrial revolution: step one, then step two, then step three. That worked in factories, but in knowledge work it leads to waiting times, handovers and layers of control.

“You end up with gatekeepers everywhere. Another check, another form. And the system gradually clogs up.”

The temptation is to replace those gatekeepers with AI. “But that’s like buying a better mop while the tap is still running.”


--- Building blocks instead of chains ---

According to Niknam, the real intervention lies in the design of the process itself. Why must work constantly be handed over? Why not organize it more asynchronously? He advocates working with modular building blocks, like a house where you can renovate one room without demolishing the entire building.

In such a system, AI is a tool, not a load-bearing wall. “AI must be replaceable. Switchable off when the context changes. If you can’t disconnect it without everything collapsing, you have a problem.

Because if the structure itself is flawed, AI won’t fix it. “It will only make things go wrong faster.”

“Don’t wait because the protocol says so”. Niknam concludes. “Act as soon as you can. And that’s exactly where AI can be a wonderful tool.”

how painful it is to build a startup.”

He cautions prospective founders against romanticizing the concept of a startup. “Make sure you actually want it and talk to people who have done it before or are doing it now.”

At the same time, he advises against inaction. “If you really want it, just go for it, because sitting on the fence for eternity is also something you shouldn’t do.”

Another important lesson for Lennard is figuring out how to handle competition — especially in a space as crowded and fast-moving as AI.

“I don’t open my LinkedIn every day anymore, because every time I do, I see another competitor,” he jokes. He explains that being confronted with competition every day is not only draining, but also distracting.

“You have to find the right balance between listening to those noises and just believe in what you’re building. There’s a lot of value in perseverance.”


=== Growing the Dutch AI ecosystem needs more work ===

Lennard contrasts the Netherlands with France, where active government support, investor focus, and ambitious infrastructure — like the soon-to-launch world’s largest startup campus — are transforming the country into Europe’s AI powerhouse.

“I feel in the last six months we’re making steps, but before that, there wasn’t a lot of noise around the ecosystem,” he says. “I think we can still do more.”

Lennard says that initiatives like KickstartAI have a role to play. He joined the community when he attended one of our events earlier this year. “I think that communities can be a big boost to the ecosystem and they’re a great way for people to feel that they are part of something.”

Post 9: 🎧 In our latest podcast, Evertjan Peer talks with Marieke Meeske (researcher at the Zero Hunger Lab at Tilburg University) about how NLP is being used in food security analysis and policymaking.

One finding from her research is hard to ignore:

Post 15: 📖 In our latest article, we take a closer look at the limits of benchmark-driven AI research – and why today’s scores often tell us less than we think.

We spoke with Maarten C. Stol, Chief Scientific Officer at Braincreators, about how benchmarks like ImageNet shaped modern AI, but also narrowed what we consider progress.

🔸 How benchmark saturation changes what accuracy scores actually represent
🔸 Why what models get wrong is more revealing than what they get right

Post 17: 🇳🇱 Today, a broad coalition of AI experts presents the National AI Delta Plan – a concrete roadmap to make the Netherlands a European leader in AI through targeted investments in knowledge, talent, and infrastructure.

At KickstartAI, we strongly support this initiative and actively contribute to its vision. For years, we’ve been working hands-on with companies across sectors to move AI from experimentation to scalable impact, while cultivating one of the Netherlands’ biggest communities of AI professionals. 

📉 The Netherlands currently ranks 23rd worldwide in AI application. Without immediate and ambitious action, we risk falling further behind, with major consequences for our competitiveness, economic security, and technological sovereignty.

We believe the AI Delta Plan is essential as it calls for: 
 ✅ Accelerating AI adoption in businesses and government
 ✅ Investing in compute infrastructure and AI labs
 ✅ Attracting and training AI talent
 ✅ Supporting AI startups and scaleups
 ✅ Establishing an independent National AI Institute
 ✅ Addressing regulatory friction that limits AI adoption and startup growth 

We are committed to contributing to this mission by accelerating responsible AI adoption, fostering talent, and strengthening collaboration across the ecosystem. The time to act is now. 

👏 Shoutout to the team behind the strategy: Jelle Prins, Michiel Bakker, Renée Frissen, Stan van Baarsen, Oscar Lepoeter, Onno Eric Blom

Read more about the National AI Delta Plan below: 

https://lnkd.in/eGPw2fRz

#AI #AINetherlands #AIDeltaPlan

Post 18: Very proud to see the CEOs of our founding partners featured in this insightful article by Het Financieele Dagblad!

📌 In this article, Frans Muller (Ahold Delhaize), Steven van Rijswijk (ING), Wouter Koolmees (Nederlandse Spoorwegen), and Marjan Rintel (KLM Royal Dutch Airlines) shared their lessons from deploying AI in large organizations: from reducing food waste and detecting unattended objects at train stations to fighting money laundering and predicting actual passenger numbers to better plan onboard services. 

What makes this piece special to us isn’t just the mention of KickstartAI, but the shared philosophy reflected: 

🔹 Focus over fragmentation
🔹 Start with the problem, not the technology
🔹 Don’t force AI on people, make it work for them
🔹 Combine operational reality with strategic vision
🔹 Move from isolated experiments to scalable practices

We’re proud of our partners, their honesty about what works (and what doesn’t), and how they keep pushing for AI that actually serves people, processes, and society.

👉 This is a must-read for any company navigating the shift from AI potentials to real operational impact.

Post 1: 🚀 Exciting news! We're kicking off the KickstartAI x ING Challenge: Financial Health Chatbot — a collaborative initiative focused on using AI to improve financial well-being.

Over the course of two weeks, 14 participants from our team and our partner companies — ING, KLM Royal Dutch Airlines, Nederlandse Spoorwegen, Ahold Delhaize, and bol — will join forces to co-create a smart, empathetic, and accessible GenAI-powered chatbot prototype.

💡 The core purpose of this challenge is to develop a tool that helps ING customers take control of their financial health, by supporting individuals experiencing financial stress or instability, as well as those actively seeking to improve their financial knowledge.

⚙️ We’ll build a solution that understands users and offers educational guidance based on validated ING content. By operating anonymously and steering clear of specific financial advice, the chatbot has the potential to lower the barriers to seeking financial support. Ultimately, it could increase financial literacy and resilience across society. 

The challenge will serve as a launchpad for validation and scaling, paving the way toward the implementation phase.

Together with our partners, we believe in sharing knowledge, building collectively, and accelerating AI adoption across the Netherlands — all while ensuring AI is used as a force for social good.🇳🇱

#innovation #GenAI #financialhealth #collaboration

Post 12: We're proud that our CEO Nils Beers represented KickstartAI at the Tweede Kamer during the roundtable discussion on the National Digitalization Strategy.

Post 14: At TechDays 2025, we’re bringing two workshops you can’t miss if you’re building (or want to build) with conversational AI and multi-agent systems. 

⚙️ Workshop 1: 
Learn how to secure conversational AI by designing, implementing, and testing guardrails that block malicious content from chatbot applications. Approaches will include task-specific ML models, LLM-based solutions, and rule-based methods. 

⚙️ Workshop 2: 
Using AGNTCY’s CoffeeAgntcy reference implementation, you’ll build and run your own multi-agent system simulating a coffee supply chain. You’ll see a supervisor agent communicate with multiple agents via the SLIM protocol and even extend the setup by adding your own servers or components. 

The workshops will be led by ML6 | Your partner in AI (powered by Cisco) — who will share real lessons from the field and guide you through the hands-on labs. Cas Coopman, Cristóbal Sendín, Thomas Vrancken, Tom Istaz, Boaz Kwakkel

👉 Spots are limited! Register for TechDays 2025 and the workshops here: https://lnkd.in/e8Y9PJDA

Post 16: We took Nvidia’s Cosmos model for a spin and created a realistic driving simulation in Amsterdam 🚘 

Advancements in machine learning show that even relatively small models (like the 7B-parameter Cosmos) can generate diverse, physically plausible synthetic data, scenarios, and even entire worlds on demand.

This capability enables developers to test AI systems across billions of virtual kilometers and countless scenarios — leading to safer, more capable AI for real-world applications like driving.

Post 3: At the Dutch AI Congress this week, we had the opportunity to share key insights on how to develop and implement AI solutions in healthcare organizations.

For the past seven months, we’ve been working closely with Erasmus MC to predict patient inflow at the emergency department — a step toward improving efficiency, capacity management, and staffing.

Our Tech Lead, Evertjan P., took the stage to talk about what it really takes to apply AI in hospital operational processes. Here are three of the most important lessons we've learned so far:

💡 Thoroughly understand and define the problem you’re aiming to solve:
This is key to creating a specific goal and a concrete plan.

💡 Assess feasibility: 
What data sources are available, and what level of performance can an AI model achieve based on these? How can we use these AI-driven insights in day-to-day practice?

💡 Determine the processes for tech integration and user adoption:
Ask: What’s the IT infrastructure like? How will the AI tool be presented to end-users? How should it be used in practice?

We’re proud to be part of the AI movement in the Netherlands — and excited for what’s next. 🧡 

A big shoutout to the AI Coalitie 4 Nederland | AIC4NL for organizing this inspiring congress!

#AI #HealthcareAI #HealthcareInnovation

Post 4: Very proud to announce that we once again joined forces with AI Coalitie 4 Nederland | AIC4NL to accelerate AI adoption in the Netherlands. Frans Muller, CEO of Ahold Delhaize, one of the founding partner companies of KickstartAI, today unveiled the second edition of the National AI Challenge, the flagship initiative of our renewed collaboration. 

The National AI Challenge was first introduced by KickstartAI and AI Coalition 4 NL in 2024, aiming to solve  concrete challenges in businesses and social organizations with artificial intelligence. The winner of the first edition, Erasmus MC, has worked with KickstartAI over the past year to improve efficiency and quality of care in the Emergency Department using AI technology.

This year’s National AI Challenge will start at the end of April and will select not one, but two use cases.

Post 4: Last Thursday, our CEO Erick Webbe joined the roundtable discussion in the Dutch Parliament (Tweede Kamer der Staten-Generaal) on how the Netherlands can make better use of the opportunities AI offers. 🇳🇱 

Our view is clear: the urgency is there – now execution needs to follow.
As Erick put it: "We need to take AI out of the ivory tower and into practice.”

That broader message was reflected in several parts of the discussion. As Maarten de Rijke also noted, the Netherlands has spent a long time thinking through the rules around AI; now it is time to start playing.

The Netherlands already has a strong foundation – from long-term research through ELLIS - European Laboratory for Learning and Intelligent Systems, to mid-term collaboration through ICAI - Innovation Center for Artificial Intelligence labs, to short-cycle implementation through initiatives like KickstartAI. What is still missing is not ideas, talent, or infrastructure, but turning that capacity into actual use.

The next step is to build on what already works. As @Petra Piersma noted, that means focusing on scaling proven solutions. 📊 

That’s the part we focus on at KickstartAI: working with organisations to apply AI in practice and sharing the learnings so they can be replicated across the Dutch ecosystem. 

We’d be happy to welcome interested MPs Claire Martens - America, Henk Vermeer, Ouafa Oualhadj, Jantine Zwinkels, Sarah El Boujdaini, Barbara Kathmann to one of our upcoming project launches and show what that real life AI solutions look like in action. 🚀  

#VVD #CDA #BBB #D66 #PRO #KickstartAI #AI

Post 6: 🚀 This month, we kicked off the first of our new Knowledge Sharing Sessions with our corporate partners: Ahold Delhaize, ING, KLM Royal Dutch Airlines, and Nederlandse Spoorwegen.

These closed-door sessions are designed to create space to share what teams are seeing, testing, and learning – so organisations don't have to work through these developments in isolation, but can build on each other’s experiences.

For this first edition, we focused on MCPs (Model Context Protocol) and AI agents, opening with a keynote by Bob van Luijt. We moved into roundtable discussions on where these building blocks are already showing up, what teams are experimenting with, and where the practical questions still are.

The goal is simple: by turning these insights into a shared knowledge base, the whole group can move forward faster. A big thank you to all the participants! 👏 

#AIAgents #MCPs #KickstartAI #AppliedAI

Post 7: We are proud to share that our CEO, Erick Webbe, will be taking part in a roundtable discussion at the Dutch Parliament (Tweede Kamer der Staten-Generaal) on 26 March about optimizing AI opportunities for the Netherlands.

At KickstartAI, our position is clear: The Netherlands 🇳🇱  has many prerequisites in place – talent, infrastructure, and a strong business climate – but we are losing momentum to low AI implementation capacity and structures that weren't built for the speed of this technology. As a result, we're not realizing the full potential of AI and its benefits for society.

Erick will be advocating for a national shift from talk to action. To do that, we need:
✅ Space for execution and a mandate for practical experimentation. 
✅ Moving past existing structures to prioritize performance and scale. 
✅ Bridging the impact gap by tackling today’s most complex challenges

We know this is possible through our own projects – from reducing food waste with Ahold Delhaize, to optimizing logistics with Ampère, and improving financial health with ING.

📖 You can find our full position paper on the Parliament’s website here: https://lnkd.in/e2X6exwK 

Looking forward to the conversation Claire Martens - America, Judith Bühler, Ouafa Oualhadj, Barbara Kathmann, Tom van der Lee, Daniël van den Berg!

Post 19: Exciting news! We’ve completed the KickstartAI x bol Challenge: Optimizing the Delivery Promise 🚚

Last month, 16 engineers from KickstartAI, bol, KLM Royal Dutch Airlines, ING, Ampère, Nederlandse Spoorwegen, and Ahold Delhaize came together for a two-week AI sprint.

💡 The focus was practical: working on a delivery prediction model with the ultimate goal to improve customer experience on bol’s platform. 

Working in mixed teams, participants tested ideas, compared approaches, and built on each other’s work in a short, hands-on setup. A visit to a bol warehouse added valuable context and helped connect the technical work to day-to-day operations. 

The challenge concluded with a closing session and drinks, wrapping up two weeks of close collaboration across organizations. Together with our partners, we believe in sharing knowledge, building collectively, and accelerating AI adoption across the Netherlands 🇳🇱

#AI #MachineLearning #AIinNL #Collaboration

In our latest article, we dive into two insightful projects: BioAnalyst, a large-scale foundation model for biodiversity forecasting, and bio-inspired computer vision to identify individual animals by the patterns on their skin.

We speak with Thanasis Trantas, AI Research Scientist at TNO, and Rita Pucci, Assistant Professor at LIACS about how these models are built, the challenges behind them, and what they could mean for the future of biodiversity research.

👇 Read the full article below:

Post 15: 📖 In our latest article, we take a closer look at the limits of benchmark-driven AI research – and why today’s scores often tell us less than we think.

We spoke with Maarten C. Stol, Chief Scientific Officer at Braincreators, about how benchmarks like ImageNet shaped modern AI, but also narrowed what we consider progress.

We dive into: 
🔸 How benchmark saturation changes what accuracy scores actually represent
🔸 Why what models get wrong is more revealing than what they get right
🔸 Shifting evaluation from performance numbers to the quality and nature of error cases

👇 Read the full article below and share your thoughts in the comments!
https://lnkd.in/euQybT5N

#AI #MachineLearning #Benchmarking

Post 17: 🤔 Should we be calling LLMs "reasoning" models?

Niya Stoimenova, who holds a PhD in AI reasoning, says the answer is no. In our new Community Spotlight, she explains why the industry's label is misleading. She breaks down deduction, induction, and abduction — and makes a compelling case for what's missing in today’s AI.

And the gap she points to? It may be the very thing that separates smart AI from real AGI. 

👉 This is a must read for anyone researching, building, or applying AI.

Post 20: ⚠️ What if we went on an AI strike for just 48 hours?
No search. No logistics. No fraud detection. No diagnostics.

That’s the idea that Nils Beers breaks down in our latest podcast to demonstrate that AI isn’t hypothetical. It’s foundational.

And that’s exactly why the EU’s AI Act matters, and why it has to get this moment right. We’re not regulating an emerging technology anymore. We’re setting the rules for systems our economy and society already depend on.

So are we writing rules that strengthen these systems — or slow them down?

🎧 Listen to the full conversation (https://lnkd.in/eMXWnPE6) and share your thoughts in the comments!

#AIAct #AI #TheKickstartAIpodcast

belief: Early‑stage AI companies must eventually choose a clear growth motion because remaining between sales‑led and product‑led strategies diffuses focus.
source_quote:
ernal API failures, and manage the non-deterministic nature of AI outputs — all while keeping the experience smooth for the user.

But the most complex problem Lleverage has faced so far isn’t a technical one.


=== The greatest early-stage challenge: finding product-market fit ===

“The biggest challenge for a company in our stage is finding the right product-market fit,” Lennard says. “It’s an iffy concept.”

“When do you feel that your product metrics are giving you the signals that your positioning is right for you? Or when do you think that there’s not enough real traction and you need to keep changing and do another iteration on the product or the business proposition?”

“It’s a constant struggle,” he adds.

Lennard also stresses that the search for product-market fit influences every part of the business: from the business model and the pricing strategy to the type of talent needed and the overall go-to-market motion.

“When transitioning to a sales-led enterprise play, for example, you need to think of a different organizational structure, with a focus on customer success, solutions engineers, and dedicated account executives,” he says.

He compares this to a product-led growth strategy, where the focus shifts towards growth marketing and community management.

Lleverage itself is now in-between the sales-led and the product-led growth motion.

“We’re consciously not making a decision right now,” Lennard explains. “But we’re going to make one in the following months. We can’t maintain this position for a long time, because it defocuses a small company like us.”

Besides market fit, Lennard mentions another challenge for early-stage companies: funding. The startup raised €3mn earlier this month, bringing its total investment to €5mn in less than a year. It has managed to avoid the pitfalls of the funding rollercoaster thanks to its seasoned founders who have already built and scaled successful companies.

But for first-time founders, attracting capital — especially in the AI space — is no easy task, Lennard says. “You have to convince VCs about the defensibility of your idea and that you can actually outgrow your competition.”


=== Building a startup is no bed of roses ===

When asked about the biggest lesson he’s learned on his journey with Lleverage, Lennard doesn’t hesitate: “I had forgotten how painful it is to build a startup.”

He cautions prospective founders against romanticizing the concept of a startup. “Make sure you actually want it and talk to people who have done it before or are doing it now.”

At the same time, he advises against inaction. “If you really want it, just go for it, because sitting on the fence for eternity is also something you shouldn’t do.”

Another important lesson for Lennard is figuring out how to handle competition — especially in a space as crowded and fast-moving as AI.

“I don’t open my LinkedIn every day anymore, because every time I do, I see another competitor,” he jokes. He explains that being confronted with competition every day is not only draining, but also distracting.

“You have to

belief: Organisations should start AI projects by using the best available model on a subset of data to establish what’s possible, then optimise for cost later.
source_quote:
del's mistakes weren't obvious hallucinations but subtle misinterpretations. One telling example: documents noted decreased CO2 emissions during COVID-19, particularly in aviation. The model cited this as evidence that pandemics worsen climate change, completely inverting the actual relationship.

Multiple mitigation strategies failed to resolve these issues:

Extensive prompt refinement

Extensive prompt refinement

Alternative chunking strategies

Alternative chunking strategies

Decomposing tasks into sequential LLM calls

Decomposing tasks into sequential LLM calls

Building comprehensive testing frameworks

Building comprehensive testing frameworks

Each fix introduced new problems. My prompts evolved into lengthy lists of prohibitions rather than clear instructions. The core issue remained: when searching for rare signals, even small error rates compound into significant noise.


>>> The bigger model solution <<<

Then Gemini 2.5 Flash (preview) arrived. At $15 cents per million tokens versus $10 cents for GPT-4o mini, it wasn't dramatically more expensive. But the performance difference was striking.

I scrapped my elaborate prompt engineering and started fresh with simple, clear instructions. It just worked. This is Rich Sutton's " bitter lesson " in action: clever engineering usually loses to approaches that better leverage computational resources. All my GPT-4o mini optimizations were essentially band-aids on a model that wasn't quite powerful enough for the task.

Lesson learned: start with the best model there is on a data subset. Establish what's possible, then optimize for cost if needed. Don't start cheap and try to engineer your way up, you'll waste time and sanity. Donald Knuth’s software engineering lessons can apply to machine learning as well.


>>> What the network revealed <<<

Among other things, the final analysis uncovered that cyber attacks, hybrid warfare, and societal polarization form a self-reinforcing triangle at the center of our threat landscape. This mirrors the recent Dutch security agency reports . It’s an  unsurprising result given that these sorts of reports also belong in our corpus, but it is a nice, if somewhat unsettling, validation check.


>>> Building the interactive visualization <<<

The network data required an interface that would make exploration intuitive while maintaining transparency about sources. I used Claude Sonnet and Gemini 2.5 Pro to help quickly develop a static website with interactive graph visualization.

The primary visualization uses Reagraph , a WebGL-based graph library. While generally excellent, it had limitations in our case. For example, the clickable area for edges was too small. Nevertheless, modern LLMs proved very useful here, helping me quickly understand the library's architecture and create a forked version with improved interaction targets. This approach — using LLMs to navigate and modify unfamiliar codebases, significantly sped up development.

When SVG export functionality became a requirement, I took a pragmatic approach. Since Reagraph's WebGL foundation made direct SVG export challenging,

Post 3: On Friday, we wrapped up our last workshop of the year with a hands-on session focused on humanitarian impact 🌎 

We teamed up with 510 - The Netherlands Red Cross' data & digital team to tackle a critical challenge: the lack of reliable data on water access and waste management in areas with limited monitoring.

Participants explored how openly available satellite imagery could help detect:
🔹 Water points such as wells, tanks, and reservoirs
🔹 Indicators of pollution or poor waste management

💡 The outcome?
Rather than building full models, the goal was to assess feasibility. Mixed teams brainstormed patterns visible from space and identified the tools needed to turn these insights into support for humanitarian and environmental action.

Post 9: 🎧 In our latest podcast, Evertjan Peer talks with Marieke Meeske (researcher at the Zero Hunger Lab at Tilburg University) about how NLP is being used in food security analysis and policymaking.

One finding from her research is hard to ignore:

Post 10: We’re excited to share that Erick Webbe will be joining KickstartAI as our new CEO starting January 1 🚀 

Erick is a familiar and trusted force within KickstartAI. As a long-time board member representing Ahold Delhaize, he has actively supported our mission to accelerate the adoption of AI in the Netherlands. He brings a deep understanding of how large organizations work, how AI moves from idea to implementation, and what it takes to connect different stakeholders around one shared goal.

In his new role as CEO, Erick will focus on growing KickstartAI’s impact: helping more organisations tackle concrete AI challenges, sharing what works openly with the community, and strengthening the Dutch AI ecosystem. 🇳🇱 

As Erick puts it, “the Netherlands must move from talking about AI to doing AI.”
“We want to show that progress doesn’t start with big words, but with tangible actions. And that a small country, if it wants to, can set a big example. I’m incredibly excited to turn our mission into reality.”

Post 15: 📖 In our latest article, we take a closer look at the limits of benchmark-driven AI research – and why today’s scores often tell us less than we think.

We spoke with Maarten C. Stol, Chief Scientific Officer at Braincreators, about how benchmarks like ImageNet shaped modern AI, but also narrowed what we consider progress.

We dive into: 
🔸 How benchmark saturation changes what accuracy scores actually represent
🔸 Why what models get wrong is more revealing than what they get right
🔸 Shifting evaluation from performance numbers to the quality and nature of error cases

Post 16: Yesterday, ING and KickstartAI - together with our partners Ahold Delhaize, KLM Royal Dutch Airlines and Nederlandse Spoorwegen - hosted the AI team of the Ministry of Economic Affairs for a working visit focused on practical AI application and adoption.

🇳🇱 KickstartAI is built on the belief that accelerating the Dutch economy through AI requires shared learning and action. During the session, our founding partners shared practical insights from the AI projects we’re carrying out together: 
🔸 What it really takes to move from idea to implementation and adoption
🔸  How to set clear goals, and how to embed AI into the core of their organisations - not as side projects, but as drivers of impact.

We discussed how organisations can strengthen their AI adoption by learning through doing, by looking at AI not only as a technology but also in terms of organisational change and broader impact, and by integrating it deeply into customer-facing and operational processes. 

We also explored how the government can help lower barriers and accelerate this transition.

⚡ Our priority at KickstartAI is clear: the urgency of AI adoption is real, and action is needed now for the Netherlands to stay competitive, economically resilient, and technologically independent.

Thanks to the Ministry for the open and constructive exchange, and to our partners for sharing their experiences so openly. The work continues!

#AI #AINetherlands #AIAdoption

Post 6: 🚀 We’re excited to kick off our project with Politie Nederland and start working on an AI tool to support the fight against environmental crime. 

While environmental crime is the third largest criminal activity in the world, the respective laws are complex and environmental criminals know how to cover their tracks. But in doing so, they’re prone to violating other laws. 

💡 That’s why the idea is to develop a tool that can search for all relevant legislation beyond the scope of environmental law, potentially uncovering criminal offences that are easier to prove. 

Think of Al Capone, who ultimately didn’t get convicted for the many murders tied to his name, but for something entirely different: tax evasion. 

The Police was one of the winners of the National AI Challenge 2025 we organized together with AI Coalitie 4 Nederland | AIC4NL. As part of the prize, our team of ML engineers will help bring their AI idea to life, from development to adoption. 

At KickstartAI, we believe AI is a force for good. We look forward to continue working with the Police on a project with such far-reaching societal impact. 

⚡ Stay tuned! 

#AIforGood #AI #AISocialImpact

Post 1: Afgelopen dinsdag organiseerden NLTech, KickstartAI en Techleap hét Nederlandse Tech verkiezingsdebat, waar kandidaat-Kamerleden en ondernemers in gesprek gingen over een toekomstbestendige digitale economie en sterk tech-ecosysteem.
 
Kandidaat-Kamerleden Wimar Bolhuis (GroenLinks-PvdA), Maes van Lanschot (CDA), Claire Martens - America (VVD), Joost Sneller (D66), Marieke Koekkoek (Volt Nederland) en Christian van der Krift, PhD (ChristenUnie) gingen het debat aan over hoe Nederland een toekomstbestendig tech ecosysteem —  van startups tot digitale kampioenen — kan bouwen en hoe we als Nederland AI-koploper kunnen worden.
 
Wat opviel tijdens de discussie over #kapitaal: vrijwel alle partijen pleitten voor meer langetermijndenken en investeringszekerheid. Niemand twijfelde aan de noodzaak om innovatie te stimuleren, maar de vraag bleef 𝘩𝘰𝘦. Er werd gepleit voor het versterken van de Europese #kapitaalunie, maar ook voor een nationale #investeringsinstelling en meer publiek-private samenwerking.
 
Onderwijs en kennisontwikkeling vormen de basis van onze toekomstige economie. Tegelijkertijd benadrukte de partijen dat fiscale regelingen als de expat-regeling cruciaal zijn voor het aantrekken van internationaal #talent, en dat aanpassing hiervan onverstandig is voor de Nederlandse concurrentiepositie binnen de EU.
 
#AI werd gezien als essentieel. VVD, GLPvdA, Volt, D66 en CU waren het eens: voor Nederland is het nu tijd voor actie om concurrerend te blijven en kansen te grijpen door snelle (en verantwoorde) AI-adoptie.

👉 Belangrijk is nu dat we deze mooie plannen en ambities terugzien in het regeerakkoord. 

#AI #AINederland

Post 19: 📣 The speaker drop for TechDays 2025 begins 📣 

We're starting off strong with Edwin Rijgersberg, ML Engineer and the creator of GEITje-7B: the first open Dutch LLM. 

GEITje-7B was hailed as important step for Dutch AI and found its way into scientific papers, paving the road for a wave of Dutch open models. But about a year after its creation, GEITje-7B also became the first model removed under threat of litigation over its training data. 

Now, as the AI Act reshapes the field, Rijgersberg will take the stage to tell the story behind GEITje-7B's rise and fall — and share how (not) to train an LLM in 2025.

Post 14: 🧠 The smartest thing humans do? Ignore most of the data.

In our latest podcast, Evertjan Peer, Cascha van Wanrooij, and Paul Kuyer explore what sets human intelligence apart from AI — and why that difference matters in practice.

AI learns by consuming massive amounts of data. But humans evolved to ignore most information, and act on the small fraction that matters the most.

The contrast goes beyond how we process information. It also shapes what we prioritize, remember, and understand, which influences how we behave. The AI systems we’re currently using have different foundations:
🔎  Current AI architectures don't have an integrated, persistent (and autobiographical) long-term memory
⚙️ Current models operate without intent or self-awareness
📉 But they’re still being used to generate decisions and influence at scale

So what kind of guardrails do we need when deploying systems that lack the constraints and instincts that shaped human cognition?

#TheKickstartAIPodcast #AI #ArtificialIntelligence

Post 15: 🧠 Is our brain a biological computer?

In our latest podcast, Evertjan Peer and Cascha van Wanrooij sit down with Paul Kuyer to unpack a core analogy that sits at the heart of the AGI debate: If the human brain is a biological computer, why can’t a digital computer eventually do all the same things and reach superhuman levels?

While the premise is plausible, the logic behind it also risks being self-reinforcing — assuming the thing it needs to prove. 

Yes, neural networks are powerful. They outperform us in specific tasks. But, at the moment at least, the technology lacks the essential traits that define human cognition: consciousness, wants, and desires.

💬 Are we anthropomorphizing models just because they speak our language? Or do the similarities between brains and machines run deeper than we’re comfortable admitting? And if they do, what kind of caution does this call for? 

👉 Listen to the full discussion here:https://lnkd.in/eFGHnCyE

#TheKickstartAIpodcast #AGI #AI

Post 16: AI holds a €85bn potential for the Dutch economy, but as Sophia Claire Zitman pointed out at TNW Conference, adoption is lagging.

It’s not about the technology, talent, or compute. It’s that AI is still stuck in the "potential" phase — too abstract, too big, too misaligned with how organizations actually work.

To unlock its impact, we have to stop treating AI like another IT upgrade. We have to rethink how we work and operate. We need to move from traditional metrics like OKRs and KPIs, to focusing on the people within organizations who are ready to reimagine what’s possible — and act on it.

In our projects, this approach has led to real results:
✈️  KLM Royal Dutch Airlines cut food waste by 65%

🚉 Nederlandse Spoorwegen train stations increasing safety

Post 7: Great news! 🚀 We collaborated with DenkWerk on their new report examining how the Netherlands can better prepare for an increasingly interconnected threat landscape.

🔎 The challenge: 
Traditional threat analysis treats risks in isolation. But today, threats are deeply intertwined. A cyberattack can disrupt supply chains. Climate events can ripple into economic shocks. As COVID showed, health crises can spark nationwide consequences.

💡 Our contribution: 
Thanks to the work of our ML Engineer Cascha van Wanrooij, we developed a node analysis tool that maps how threats connect and amplify each other.

We used LLMs to extract causal relationships from thousands of government and research documents — creating a semantic network of interlinked national threats. The result: an interactive graph that helps policymakers explore these complex threat relationships visually, moving beyond traditional "probability × impact" models to include interconnectedness as a crucial third dimension. (Link to our tool: https://lnkd.in/dqej7mDQ)

👉 Read the full report here: https://lnkd.in/d7ABqq7f

belief: Benchmark‑driven AI research that focuses on incremental accuracy gains can obscure important qualitative error patterns and limit real understanding.
source_quote:
o points to a related concern: the familiar graphs often shown in AI talks, where model accuracy rises steadily and gradually approaches the horizontal “human performance” line.

Consider, for instance, an image-classification task such as detecting cancer in X-ray scans. “Let's say human performance is at 90%, while a machine learning model performs higher, at 92%,” he says.

Looking at the numbers alone, the conclusion is that the model is better at detecting cancer than a human doctor.

But this, according to Maarten, is misleading. He argues that the most revealing evaluation of performance isn’t what a model or a human did right. It’s what they did wrong.

If a human doctor, for example, misclassifies 10% of the cases, the most likely reason behind this misclassification would be that the examples are too ambiguous or extremely difficult. When a model, however, fails in 8% of the cases, some of them would also be challenging – but others would be obvious mistakes that a trained professional would rarely, if ever, make.

“The fact that the model scores better on the frequency of a benchmark doesn’t mean that it has visual understanding, or that it understands what it’s doing,” Maarten notes. “It only means that it scores well on that particular benchmark.”

“If we combine this with the problem of differentiation, we’re eventually running out of interesting error cases. These are the cases that show us how a model makes mistakes and what we should improve.”

“I think that the sometimes unhealthy benchmarking culture, the hunting for incremental results, only focuses on how much you're doing right. But it's not looking at the quality and the nature of the mistakes that you're making.”


>>> The quest for meaning <<<

But then the question is: if we move from quantity into quality, how will we be able to evaluate it? After all, quality is about interpretation, which is inherently linked to subjectivity – a non-fitting element for science.

“We’d need another leap like ImageNet,” Stol says. “One that would come not only with millions of images, but also with hundreds of thousands of  interpretations and a specific, statistical distribution of these text descriptions in the latent space, in their vector database, and so on.”

This direction could be plausible, he adds, but it would require significant research focus. From his side, Stol is researching what he calls “notions of hardness:” what makes a problem or instance hard.

Stol comes back to the X-ray example. What he’s looking for is an explanation of what makes it hard for a machine learning model to accurately identify the scans.

“I think we need to push machine learning research into this sort of area where we start to understand these error cases we’re left with, that 8% that we never solve,” he says.

“What do these error cases have in common? Can we start to group them? Can we start to generate them? Can we start to control them? Because if we can have controlled notions of hardness, we can actually 'benchmark' on meaning instead of on frequency, accuracy, and scores.”

Stol links this to the idea of “model provenance” as proposed in “ A Hierarchy of Limitations in Machine Le

Public space data, commercial satellite imagery, and recent advances in AI are expanding what’s possible with Earth observation – both in how data is accessed and how it’s analysed.

In our latest article, we look at current and evolving AI uses in EO and examine different EO markets and applications, from climate monitoring and biodiversity assessment to regional policy-making and emerging commercial use cases. 

We spoke with Parya Pasha Zadeh (Program Manager at NL Space Campus) and Thijs Oosterhuis (Solutions Architect for Geo and Remote Sensing at @ Provincie Zuid-Holland) about how these developments are unfolding in practice, drawing on perspectives from applied EO work in the Netherlands.
