Post 18: ⚠️ What are we going to do to start AI adoption tomorrow?
That’s the key question our CEO Nils Beers posed during his speech at the Netherlands’ Tech Election Debate we co-organized last week.

At KickstartAI, we believe AI isn’t a blocker — it’s a force for good. It can safeguard our welfare state, boost our economy, and strengthen our society. The urgency is clear: if we hesitate, the Netherlands risks falling behind in competitiveness and sovereignty.

That’s why there’s no more time for endless roundtable talks, boardroom discussions, and debates in The Hague. 

It’s time to:
🔸Boost public investment by supporting frontrunners from business and from the tech ecosystem
🔸Adopt rules that support entrepreneurs instead of overburdening them with compliance and uncertainty
🔸Focus on measurable impact instead of AI pilots
🔸Create space for the doers and enable them to share their learnings

🚀 We need to act now. 
🤝 Let’s build a coalition of doing, together.

#AI #AIadoption #Coalitionofthedoing #AIadoptieNederland

Post 3: At the Dutch AI Congress this week, we had the opportunity to share key insights on how to develop and implement AI solutions in healthcare organizations.

For the past seven months, we’ve been working closely with Erasmus MC to predict patient inflow at the emergency department — a step toward improving efficiency, capacity management, and staffing.

Our Tech Lead, Evertjan P., took the stage to talk about what it really takes to apply AI in hospital operational processes. Here are three of the most important lessons we've learned so far:

💡 Thoroughly understand and define the problem you’re aiming to solve:
This is key to creating a specific goal and a concrete plan.

💡 Assess feasibility: 
What data sources are available, and what level of performance can an AI model achieve based on these? How can we use these AI-driven insights in day-to-day practice?

💡 Determine the processes for tech integration and user adoption:
Ask: What’s the IT infrastructure like? How will the AI tool be presented to end-users? How should it be used in practice?

We’re proud to be part of the AI movement in the Netherlands — and excited for what’s next. 🧡 

A big shoutout to the AI Coalitie 4 Nederland | AIC4NL for organizing this inspiring congress!

#AI #HealthcareAI #HealthcareInnovation

Post 6: ✨ We have our winners! ✨

We’re thrilled to announce that Leiden University Medical Center (via its Cairelab) and Politie Nederland have won the National AI Challenge 2025, a unique collaboration between KickstartAI and AI Coalitie 4 Nederland | AIC4NL. 

🏆 Leiden University Medical Center impressed the jury with their idea to use AI to generate structured wound reports from photos, aiming to ease the administrative burden on nurses and improve the quality of care.

"We're super happy we won this challenge! We hope that  this solution becomes a standard support tool in the wound care process — available to every nurse, in every care setting," said Albert Simonse, Nursing Information Officer at LUMC. 

🏆 Politie Nederland stood out with their vision to use AI to cut through complex environmental legislation and help investigators leverage all available laws so that they can hold polluters accountable.

"I'm very happy that we won! This AI tool will greatly help strengthen our capabilities, enabling us to take down the biggest environmental criminals faster and more decisively," said Amir Niknam, Senior Advisor at the Police. 

🔜 Over the coming months, our team will work closely with both LUMC and the Police to bring their ideas to life — from concept to fully working AI solutions.

A huge congratulations to our winners, and a heartfelt thanks to our other outstanding finalists (Gemeente Den Haag, Instituut Verantwoord Medicijngebruik, Leiden University Medical Center via its Emergency Department) for their dedication and vision! 

👏 A big shoutout to our expert jury as well (Marelle van Beerschoten, Thomas Dohmen, Thomas Faber, Herman Kienhuis, Janneke Niessen) for their contribution! 

👉 For more info, check out our press release: https://lnkd.in/eKzhzawG

ing effect.”

“There is a cost to regulatory compliance,” he says, “but if we agree that artificial intelligence will be one of the major drivers of societal change in the coming decades, it's impossible to imagine a reality where it won't be regulated.”


--- Call for support and open dialogue ---

Earlier in July, Prosus — alongside major European (and Dutch) companies including ASML, Philips, Cradle, and Mistral, led by the EU AI Champions initiative  — signed an open letter , calling the Commission for a two-year delay of the Act.

“We welcomed the call from the European Commission to provide our views on simplification of the EU AI Act and other digital laws — and we responded with concrete proposals,”  Tomczak-Górlikowska  explains.

“We signed the open letter not because we want the Act to go away for a while, but because we thought that a pause is a beneficial moment to assess some opportunities, simplify some of the requirements, and have a refreshed version aligned with the novel character of the technology and the advancements organizations have made in AI governance.”

The EU is expected to introduce its Digital Omnibus Package and Digital Fitness Check (aimed at streamlining and simplifying multiple digital laws) in the second half of 2025.

Beyond simplifying the AI Act’s requirements, Beers is also calling for greater support from national governments and the EU to help companies navigate the regulation, provide clear guidance, and offer financial assistance.

He argues that innovation-friendly regulation should be agile and fast, focused less on rigid processes and more on preventing actual harm.

“If you look at the vast majority of algorithms being applied today, 95% to 99% of the AI we’re using is designed to improve people’s lives,” he says. “It's about healthcare, it's about efficiency, it's about greening the economy, it's about reducing poverty.”

And precisely because of AI’s huge benefit to society , Beers believes that European companies have a strong negotiating power and the right to ask for regulation changes. “Imagine what would happen if we had an AI strike tomorrow,” he says.

“If we paused AI, if we closed data centers even for a couple of days, the whole society would come to a stop. I think it would show that AI isn’t a concept — it’s a fundamental part of our life, and if we change the rules around it, we need to make sure that we’re actually making things better.”

From the Dutch government's perspective, preparations for the AI Act are already underway, led by the Ministry of Economic Affairs. This includes setting up key oversight mechanisms, such as the national market surveillance authority and a single national contact point. At the same time, the government is investing in awareness and education initiatives — both within ministries and across the broader industry.

“We’re doing a lot of work not only to educate people about the AI Act, but also facilitating organizations in compliance, and invest in ongoing deliberations with Brussels on specifying certain open elements in the regulation,” Van Leeuwen says.
